inventory.name: memory-admin.sh
inventory.description: Top-level dispatcher for the memory-admin CLI (init/add/list/search/delete/stats/export/import/prune). Agents invoke it directly and it is also shelled out to by memory-sync.sh as the backing store.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage to stdout, 38 lines (verified live: `timeout 10 bash .claude/scripts/memory-admin.sh --help`). `-h` and bare `help` route to the identical case branch (main() at memory-admin.sh:891-893) so behavior is the same though not independently re-invoked.
inventory.json_mode: partial: no --json flag anywhere; JSON is the *default* raw output for list/search/stats/export/add-summary, but init/delete/prune(non-dry-run) emit plain text or nothing on stdout
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Clean split: log_info/log_success/log_warn/log_error (memory-admin.sh:58-72) ALL write to stderr via `>&2`; only command data (JSON/CSV) goes to stdout. ANSI colors are tty-guarded: `if [[ -t 1 ]]` (memory-admin.sh:44) sets escape codes only on a real terminal, else empty strings (memory-admin.sh:51) — safe for piping.
inventory.exit_code_notes: No documented exit-code dictionary in cmd_help (memory-admin.sh:812-853). Every failure path is a blanket `exit 1` (e.g. memory-admin.sh:77,90,146,155,237,272,278,295,564,663,727,732,737,897); success is always the implicit exit 0 of the last command run.
error_samples: "Database not initialized. Run: memory-admin.sh init" (memory-admin.sh:76)
"Unknown command: $command" (memory-admin.sh:895, exit at 897)
worst_suspects: self_documentation: cmd_help (812-853) never mentions the 4 env-var overrides (LOA_DIR, DB_FILE, EMBED_SCRIPT, CONFIG_FILE) that silently redirect the whole DB — no in-CLI pointer for an agent debugging 'why is my data missing'.

determinism_and_reproducibility: blanket exit 1 for every failure class means a caller branching on $? cannot tell init-missing from bad-args from not-found; must string-match stderr text that could change wording later.

inventory.name: init
inventory.description: Creates $LOA_DIR and the sqlite schema (memories/memory_embeddings/query_cache/search_history). Idempotent (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS). Every other subcommand's ensure_db_exists gate requires this to have run first.
inventory.help_probe: not independently probed — would create .loa-state/memory.db as a real side effect, outside the allowed --help/-h/help-only probe set; behavior read from source (cmd_init, memory-admin.sh:220-239).
inventory.json_mode: no — logs a human sentence via log_success/log_info (stderr); nothing structured is emitted
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: All output (log_info/log_success/log_error) goes to stderr; on success nothing is printed to stdout at all — init is stdout-silent on the happy path.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 only if the schema creation somehow leaves DB_FILE missing (memory-admin.sh:236-237); otherwise implicit exit 0.
error_samples: "Failed to create database" (memory-admin.sh:236, exit at 237) — generic, no underlying sqlite3 error text surfaced
evidence: memory-admin.sh:74-79 ensure_db_exists gate used by every other subcommand
worst_suspects: output_parseability: success path prints nothing to stdout — an agent greping stdout for confirmation gets a false negative and may re-run init unnecessarily.

inventory.name: add
inventory.description: Adds one memory: positional <content> + required --type + optional --source. Sanitizes content (strips control chars/ANSI, truncates 2000 chars), generates an embedding via a python subprocess, inserts via parameterized SQL, prints a one-line JSON summary.
inventory.help_probe: not probed live (writes a DB row — a real mutation, outside the --help/-h/help-only allowance); read from source (cmd_add, memory-admin.sh:241-348).
inventory.json_mode: yes: final stdout line is `{"id":N,"content_hash":"...","type":"..."}` (memory-admin.sh:347) — progress logging is interleaved on stderr in the same invocation, so stdout alone is clean JSON but combined stdout+stderr capture is not.
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: log_info/log_success on stderr; final JSON on stdout (memory-admin.sh:347). Correct split.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 for: missing content (270/272), missing --type (276/277), invalid --type (88-90), missing embedding script (144/145), embedding service unavailable (153-155), embedding generation failure (292-295). All indistinguishable by exit code alone.
error_samples: "Content is required" (memory-admin.sh:270)
"Memory type is required (--type)" (memory-admin.sh:276)
"Embedding service not available" / "Install: pip install sentence-transformers" (memory-admin.sh:153-154)
worst_suspects: intent_inference: the positional-arg parser (247-266) silently space-joins ANY unrecognized token onto `content` (line 261 `content="$content $1"`) instead of erroring — `add "foo" --type gotcha extra-typo-flag` silently appends 'extra-typo-flag' to the stored memory rather than raising an unknown-flag error.

inventory.name: search
inventory.description: Embeds <query>, computes cosine similarity in python against all stored embeddings, returns top-k above --threshold as a JSON array to stdout, and logs the search to search_history.
inventory.help_probe: not probed live (writes to search_history — a mutation); read from source (cmd_search, memory-admin.sh:421-556).
inventory.json_mode: yes: unconditional JSON array on stdout (memory-admin.sh:533, `json.dumps(results, indent=2)`) — no flag needed
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: log_info explicitly redirected to stderr (memory-admin.sh:457, `>&2`); JSON result to stdout. Clean split.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 for missing query (448-451) or embedding failure (461-464); zero matching results is NOT an error — exits 0 with `[]`, so agents must inspect array length, not $?.
error_samples: "Query is required" (memory-admin.sh:449)
worst_suspects: agent_ergonomics: positional query args are space-joined the same way as `add` (memory-admin.sh:437-445) — multi-word queries need quoting, but unquoted concatenation silently 'works', masking the requirement until a query happens to contain a flag-like word.

inventory.name: list
inventory.description: Lists memories (optionally filtered by --type), 80-char preview, ordered by created_at desc, default --limit 20, via a parameterized python query; prints a JSON array to stdout.
inventory.help_probe: not probed (opens the sqlite DB via ensure_db_exists — outside the --help-only allowance); read from source (cmd_list, memory-admin.sh:350-419).
inventory.json_mode: yes: unconditional JSON array (memory-admin.sh:417)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: no logging at all in cmd_list — stdout carries only the JSON array, even for zero results.
inventory.exit_code_notes: only documented failure is missing DB (ensure_db_exists, exit 1); a non-numeric --limit is coerced by python `int()` with no try/except (memory-admin.sh:381), unlike cmd_prune's explicit regex validation of its analogous numeric input (memory-admin.sh:725-733).
error_samples: "Database not initialized. Run: memory-admin.sh init" (memory-admin.sh:76) via ensure_db_exists
uncaught `ValueError: invalid literal for int()` traceback on non-numeric --limit (memory-admin.sh:381, no input validation)
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: an invalid --limit produces a raw Python traceback instead of the CLI's own log_error style — an inconsistent error surface an agent parser can't reliably pattern-match against the rest of the tool's errors.

inventory.name: delete
inventory.description: Deletes a memory by numeric id after validating it's a positive integer; reports NOT_FOUND vs DELETED via a python subprocess.
inventory.help_probe: not probed (mutates DB); read from source (cmd_delete, memory-admin.sh:558-608).
inventory.json_mode: no — success/failure both reported via log_success/log_error text on stderr; stdout is always empty
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Entirely stderr for both outcomes; stdout is always empty for this subcommand, success or failure.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 for missing id (561-564), non-numeric id (568-570), and not-found (602-604); exit 0 (implicit) for successful delete — 3 distinct causes collapse to the same exit 1.
error_samples: "Invalid memory ID: must be a positive integer" (memory-admin.sh:569)
"Memory with ID $id not found" (memory-admin.sh:603)
worst_suspects: output_parseability: success has zero stdout output and only a stderr sentence — an agent wanting to confirm 'was N actually deleted' has no machine-checkable signal besides exit code plus free-text stderr scraping.

inventory.name: prune
inventory.description: Bulk-deletes memories matching --older-than DAYS and/or --min-matches N (AND-combined), with a --dry-run preview mode; the only subcommand with an explicit safety valve, but that valve is opt-in, not default.
inventory.help_probe: not probed (bulk-deletes with no undo when run without --dry-run); read from source (cmd_prune, memory-admin.sh:697-810).
inventory.json_mode: partial: the full python result (`{"action":...,"count":...}` or with a `memories` array) is captured into a bash variable via command substitution (memory-admin.sh:742) and never echoed whole to stdout; only the dry_run branch re-emits a sub-slice, `echo "$result" | jq '.memories'` (memory-admin.sh:804), to stdout.
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: 'none' and 'deleted' branches print nothing to stdout at all (only a stderr sentence via log_info/log_success at 800/807); 'dry_run' emits only the `.memories` array (not the wrapping action/count object) to stdout.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 for invalid --older-than / --min-matches (725-727, 730-732) and for specifying neither condition (735-737); both real deletions and dry-runs exit 0 with no exit-code distinction between 'nothing pruned' and 'pruned successfully'.
error_samples: "At least one condition required: --older-than DAYS or --min-matches N" (memory-admin.sh:736)
"Invalid --older-than value: must be a positive integer" (memory-admin.sh:726)
evidence: memory-admin.sh:786-788 real DELETE with no confirmation prompt
memory-admin.sh:742 result captured via command substitution, never echoed whole
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery: the only irreversible bulk-DELETE command in the family defaults to LIVE execution — --dry-run must be explicitly opted into (memory-admin.sh:712-715); a copy-pasted prune command missing -n/--dry-run permanently deletes matching rows with no confirmation, backup, or undo path.

output_parseability: an agent scripting around `prune` to confirm how many rows were deleted gets NOTHING on stdout for the real-delete path (count is swallowed inside the command-substituted $result and only surfaced as a stderr sentence at memory-admin.sh:807) — the count is only machine-readable in dry-run mode, and even then only via a `.memories` array length, not a `.count` field.

inventory.name: LOA_DIR (and sibling DB_FILE/EMBED_SCRIPT/CONFIG_FILE)
inventory.description: Overridable data-directory root; defaults to ${PROJECT_ROOT}/.loa-state (relocated from .loa/ in cycle-035 to avoid submodule collision per the code comment). Tests/operators redirect the whole DB by setting this before invocation.
inventory.help_probe: n/a (env var, not shown by --help)
inventory.json_mode: n/a
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a
inventory.exit_code_notes: n/a — silently changes which DB file every subsequent subcommand reads/writes; an unset vs. mistyped override both fall through to the default path with no validation or echo of 'using LOA_DIR=X' anywhere.
worst_suspects: self_documentation: none of the 4 override vars (LOA_DIR, DB_FILE, EMBED_SCRIPT, CONFIG_FILE) are mentioned in cmd_help (memory-admin.sh:812-853) — an agent hunting for 'why is my memory DB empty after a fresh clone' has to read source, not the CLI's own docs.

inventory.name: db_not_initialized (ensure_db_exists gate)
inventory.description: Shared guard called at the top of 8 subcommands (add, list, search, delete, stats, export, import, prune) — centralizes the 'have you run init' check.
inventory.help_probe: n/a
inventory.json_mode: no — plain stderr text
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: stderr only (log_error, memory-admin.sh:71 function def; call site 76).
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1, identical to every other validation failure in the script (no distinct code for 'not initialized' vs 'bad input').
error_samples: "Database not initialized. Run: memory-admin.sh init" (memory-admin.sh:76)
evidence: call sites: cmd_add(281), cmd_list(371), cmd_search(454), cmd_delete(573), cmd_stats(611), cmd_export(641), cmd_import(677), cmd_prune(722)
worst_suspects: regression_resistance: 8 separate call sites duplicate the same gate call with no shared retry/auto-init suggestion beyond the string — a future change to the init flow requires updating a message repeated identically at 8 sites.

inventory.name: qmd-sync.sh
inventory.description: Dispatcher for sync/status/create/delete/query against QMD (semantic doc search) collections defined in .loa.config.yaml; falls back to grep-based search when the `qmd` binary isn't installed.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage to stdout, 32 lines (verified live: `timeout 10 bash .claude/scripts/qmd-sync.sh --help`).
inventory.json_mode: partial: `query` returns a JSON array (qmd-native or grep-fallback) on stdout; `status`/`sync`/`create`/`delete` are human-text only
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: INCONSISTENT vs. the other two scripts in this family: log_info (qmd-sync.sh:58-60) and log_warn (62-64) write to STDOUT (no `>&2`); only log_error (66-68) goes to stderr. Combined with unconditional, non-tty-guarded ANSI color codes (49-52), `query`/`status`/`sync` can interleave raw `\033[...]m` escapes and human sentences into the SAME stdout stream that also carries JSON — e.g. cmd_query's disabled-config path prints a colored '[WARN] QMD is disabled' line (stdout) immediately before `echo "[]"` (also stdout, 559-561), so `qmd-sync.sh query x | jq .` on a disabled config fails to parse.
inventory.exit_code_notes: blanket exit 1 for missing args (536,545,554) and unknown command (632-634); QMD-disabled or binary-missing paths deliberately `return 0` (e.g. cmd_sync:460-463, cmd_query:558-562) so $? alone cannot distinguish 'ran successfully' from 'silently did nothing because disabled'.
error_samples: "Usage: qmd-sync.sh create <name>" (qmd-sync.sh:536)
"SECURITY: Config index_dir contains path traversal, using default" (qmd-sync.sh:109, non-fatal — script substitutes a default and continues rather than failing)
evidence: qmd-sync.sh:49-52 unguarded ANSI colors (contrast memory-admin.sh:44-52 tty guard)
worst_suspects: composability: log_info/log_warn on stdout (58-64) plus unguarded ANSI (49-52) means this script's stdout is NOT safely pipeable into jq/grep the way memory-admin.sh's is — the exact opposite convention from its sibling script in the same family, silently breaking output for an agent that learned the safe pattern there.

inventory.name: sync
inventory.description: Iterates configured collections (memory.qmd.collections), indexes matching files via mtime-cache incremental tracking (or --force full), and shells out to the `qmd` binary if present (collection add + update).
inventory.help_probe: not probed (real filesystem side effects: mkdir + mtime cache writes + optional `qmd` binary invocation); read from source (cmd_sync, qmd-sync.sh:446-499).
inventory.json_mode: no — plain `log_info "Sync complete: N collections processed"` sentence, no structured summary
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: log_info calls land on stdout per the script-level routing — 'Syncing collection: X' / 'Sync complete: N collections processed' are mixed with any ANSI codes.
inventory.exit_code_notes: returns 0 in ALL observed paths — even 'QMD is disabled' (460-463) and 'No collections configured' (470-473) are treated as success, so a scheduler relying on exit code can never detect a no-op sync.
error_samples: "QMD is disabled or config missing" (qmd-sync.sh:461, exit 0 despite the wording)
worst_suspects: intent_inference: a misconfigured or disabled QMD setup makes `sync` a silent no-op that still exits 0 — an agent wiring this into a session-start hook cannot detect 'sync never actually ran' without parsing stdout text.

inventory.name: status
inventory.description: Prints QMD enabled/disabled, binary availability, index dir, and a per-collection indexed-file-count summary — the primary 'is this working' diagnostic command.
inventory.help_probe: not probed live (load_config can emit SECURITY stderr as a side effect of reading the config — excluded from the --help-only rule); read from source (cmd_status, qmd-sync.sh:501-531).
inventory.json_mode: no — plain-text report only, no --json/-o json option, unlike memory-admin's `stats` which uses `sqlite3 -json`
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: all `echo` lines here (507-529) go straight to stdout with no log_ wrapper at all.
inventory.exit_code_notes: always returns 0, even when QMD is fully disabled (502-505) — 'status' can never signal failure via exit code.
evidence: qmd-sync.sh:508 binary-availability check inline in echo
worst_suspects: output_parseability: free-text report with no machine-readable mode at all — an agent wanting 'is qmd enabled: true/false' must regex `QMD Status: (ENABLED|DISABLED)` out of prose, the only status-style command in the family without a JSON option.

inventory.name: query
inventory.description: Searches across all configured collections (query_all -> query_collection), using the `qmd` binary's `search --format json` when available, else a grep-based fallback that manually assembles JSON strings via jq/sed string surgery.
inventory.help_probe: not probed (loads config + touches index dir); read from source (cmd_query 551-565, query_all 410-440, query_collection 344-407).
inventory.json_mode: yes when it works — degrades to plain `[]` on subprocess failure (qmd-sync.sh:363, `... --format json 2>/dev/null || echo "[]"`), so a broken `qmd` binary is indistinguishable from a genuinely empty result set
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: same stdout-pollution risk as the script-level surface: the disabled-config path emits a stdout log_warn line before the `[]` (qmd-sync.sh:559-561).
inventory.exit_code_notes: always exits 0 whether results were found, none were found, QMD is disabled, or the underlying `qmd search` subprocess itself failed (363's `|| echo "[]"` swallows the real exit code) — $? cannot distinguish a broken `qmd` binary from an empty corpus.
error_samples: "Usage: qmd-sync.sh query <query>" (qmd-sync.sh:554, the only real failure mode)
evidence: qmd-sync.sh:357-363 qmd binary search with swallowed stderr+exit code
qmd-sync.sh:390-397 grep-fallback JSON built via sed/jq string surgery
worst_suspects: determinism_and_reproducibility: the `qmd` binary path and the grep-fallback path return structurally different score semantics for the identical query (qmd assigns real scores; fallback hardcodes `"score":0.5` at qmd-sync.sh:396) — the same query against the same corpus returns different result shapes purely based on whether the `qmd` binary happens to be installed, with no mode/version field in the output to tell them apart.

inventory.name: create
inventory.description: mkdir -p's a new empty collection directory under $QMD_DIR/<name>; does NOT register it in .loa.config.yaml, so a created collection is invisible to sync/query/status until an operator manually adds the corresponding entry to memory.qmd.collections.
inventory.help_probe: not probed (mkdir side effect); read from source (cmd_create 533-539, create_collection 228-234).
inventory.json_mode: no — one log_info sentence
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: log_info to stdout per script-level routing.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 only for missing name arg (535-537); otherwise always exit 0, even though the created dir is functionally inert until config-registered.
error_samples: "Usage: qmd-sync.sh create <name>" (qmd-sync.sh:536)
evidence: qmd-sync.sh:228-234 create_collection body — no config-file mutation anywhere in this path
qmd-sync.sh:218-225 get_collections reads only .loa.config.yaml, never the QMD_DIR filesystem
worst_suspects: intent_inference: the name 'create a collection' strongly implies it becomes usable, but `create` never touches .loa.config.yaml's memory.qmd.collections list that sync/query/status actually read (get_collections, 218-225) — an agent that runs `create foo` then `query` expecting foo to be searched gets silently nothing, with no warning that a config edit is still required.

inventory.name: delete
inventory.description: rm -rf's the collection directory under $QMD_DIR/<name> (no config-file cleanup either, mirroring create's registration gap).
inventory.help_probe: not probed (destructive rm -rf, no confirmation); read from source (cmd_delete 542-549, delete_collection 237-247).
inventory.json_mode: no — log_info/log_warn sentence only
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: stdout per script-level log routing.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 only for missing name (544-546); 'collection not found' is a WARN not an error and still exits 0 (delete_collection:244-246), so a typo'd collection name silently no-ops instead of failing.
error_samples: "Usage: qmd-sync.sh delete <name>" (qmd-sync.sh:545)
"Collection not found: $name" (qmd-sync.sh:245, exit 0 — not treated as an error)
evidence: qmd-sync.sh:237-247 delete_collection — unconditional `rm -rf "$collection_dir"` with no confirmation prompt and no dry-run option
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery: unconditional `rm -rf "$collection_dir"` (qmd-sync.sh:242) with zero confirmation, no --dry-run equivalent, and no trash/backup — the only other bulk-delete in the family (memory-admin prune) at least offers --dry-run; this one doesn't, despite being equally irreversible.

inventory.name: QMD_DIR (+ _QMD_DIR_ENV precedence)
inventory.description: Overrides the index directory; the script captures a pre-set QMD_DIR into `_QMD_DIR_ENV` BEFORE reading the config file so a trusted operator/test override always wins over the (security-sanitized) config-file value — a deliberate trust-boundary design documented inline.
inventory.help_probe: n/a (env var)
inventory.json_mode: n/a
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a
inventory.exit_code_notes: n/a — but interacts with the SECURITY validation path: when QMD_DIR is not pre-set, a config index_dir containing `..`, a leading `/`, or shell metacharacters triggers a `log_error "SECURITY: ..."` stderr line and a silent fallback to the default, rather than aborting — the script continues running with substituted config instead of failing loudly.
error_samples: "SECURITY: Config index_dir contains path traversal, using default" (qmd-sync.sh:109)
"SECURITY: Config index_dir should be relative, using default" (qmd-sync.sh:113)
"SECURITY: Config index_dir contains shell metacharacters, using default" (qmd-sync.sh:117)
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: all three SECURITY-labeled messages are worded like alerts but are non-fatal (script substitutes a default and keeps going) — an agent scanning logs for 'SECURITY' during a routine sync may assume the run aborted, when it actually proceeded with different config than requested.

inventory.name: qmd_binary_unavailable (check_qmd_available, silent)
inventory.description: The check for whether the `qmd` binary is installed prints NOTHING on failure — no warning, no log line — before every caller silently switches to a degraded grep-based search or skips QMD registration. The one 'error condition' in the family with zero user-facing message at its point of detection.
inventory.help_probe: n/a — this is an absence of a message, not something a --help probe would surface
inventory.json_mode: n/a
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a — nothing is emitted at the detection site (qmd-sync.sh:129-134); each of the 3 call sites (cmd_status:508, index_collection:290, query_collection:357) independently decides what, if anything, to say afterward.
inventory.exit_code_notes: check_qmd_available returns 1 (a bash function return, not a script exit) — consumed silently by every `if check_qmd_available; then ... fi` call site, never surfaced to the script's own exit code.
error_samples: (none — qmd-sync.sh:129-134 check_qmd_available() has zero log_* calls in its body; the only user-facing hint is cmd_status's inline `$(check_qmd_available && echo "available" || echo "NOT FOUND")` at line 508)
worst_suspects: agent_intuitiveness: an agent running `sync` or `query` on a machine without the `qmd` binary installed gets a fully successful-looking run (exit 0, plausible output) via the grep fallback — the only hint is `status`'s 'NOT FOUND' line, which the agent must think to check separately since sync/query never mention degraded mode.

inventory.name: memory-sync.sh
inventory.description: Higher-level sync orchestrator built on top of memory-admin.sh: extracts '## Learnings' bullets from NOTES.md (or JSON/markdown retrospective output) and forwards each as a memory-admin.sh `add` call, deduping via a memory-admin.sh `search --threshold 0.9` first.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage to stdout, 23 lines (verified live: `timeout 10 bash .claude/scripts/memory-sync.sh --help`).
inventory.json_mode: no — this script emits no JSON of its own; it only consumes JSON returned by the memory-admin.sh subprocess calls it shells out to
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Consistent with memory-admin.sh (unlike qmd-sync.sh): log_info/log_success/log_warn/log_error (memory-sync.sh:43-57) ALL write to stderr; there is no stdout data channel for this script at all — it is a pure side-effecting orchestrator.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 for unknown command (431-433) and when check_memory_admin fails inside sync_notes/sync_retrospective (106-118 gate, propagated at 172/263); auto_sync's 'disabled in config' path (319-321) is a `return 0`, deliberately not an error.
error_samples: "memory-admin.sh not found" (memory-sync.sh:108, returns 1)
"Memory database not initialized. Run: memory-admin.sh init" (memory-sync.sh:113, WARN-level despite gating a hard stop)
"No input provided. Pipe retrospective output or provide file." (memory-sync.sh:273, returns 1)
worst_suspects: regression_resistance: this script shells out to `memory-admin.sh search ... | jq 'length'` (memory-sync.sh:211,291) to detect duplicates — any future change to memory-admin's search output shape (e.g. wrapping the array in an object) silently breaks dedup detection here, with no contract test coupling the two scripts.

inventory.name: notes
inventory.description: Reads NOTES.md, extracts lines under an exact '## Learnings' heading (awk section capture), parses [GOTCHA]/[PATTERN]/[DECISION] tags into memory_type, hash-gates re-processing via a stored content hash, dedupes each candidate via memory-admin search, then calls memory-admin add for new ones.
inventory.help_probe: not probed (mutates .loa-state/sync_state.json and the memory DB); read from source (sync_notes, memory-sync.sh:168-233).
inventory.json_mode: no — logs "Synced N learnings from NOTES.md (skipped M)" as text only
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: entirely stderr, per script-level log routing.
inventory.exit_code_notes: returns 0 even for the two common no-op cases ('NOTES.md not found' at 175-177, 'unchanged since last sync' at 186-189) — a caller can't distinguish 'synced 0 because nothing changed' from 'synced 0 because NOTES.md doesn't exist' via exit code, only by reading differently-worded log lines.
error_samples: "NOTES.md not found: $NOTES_FILE" (memory-sync.sh:176, exit 0 — logged as WARN)
"Failed to add: ${content:0:50}..." (memory-sync.sh:224, non-fatal — loop continues)
worst_suspects: agent_ergonomics: the entire feature depends on NOTES.md having an EXACT '## Learnings' heading (extract_learnings' awk pattern at memory-sync.sh:134 is a literal-string match) — any heading variant ('## Learning', '##Learnings', '## learnings') silently yields zero extracted lines with no warning that the heading wasn't found, only the generic downstream 'Synced 0 learnings' success message.

inventory.name: retrospective
inventory.description: Accepts retrospective output via stdin pipe or a file argument, auto-detects JSON vs markdown-bullet format, extracts type|content pairs, and forwards each to memory-admin.sh add (same dedup pattern as notes).
inventory.help_probe: not probed (mutates memory DB via subprocess adds); read from source (sync_retrospective, memory-sync.sh:260-307).
inventory.json_mode: no — text summary only ('Extracted N memories...')
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: stderr only, consistent with the rest of this script.
inventory.exit_code_notes: returns 1 if neither stdin is a pipe nor a valid file arg is given (268-274); otherwise 0 regardless of how many memories were actually extracted, even 0.
error_samples: "No input provided. Pipe retrospective output or provide file." (memory-sync.sh:273)
worst_suspects: intent_inference: format auto-detection (`jq -e '.'` at memory-sync.sh:245) treats ANY valid-JSON input as the 'JSON format' branch even if it lacks the expected .skills[] shape — a differently-shaped JSON blob parses as valid, takes the JSON branch, and the `.skills // [] | .[]` filter (248-249) yields nothing, producing a silent 'Extracted 0 memories' instead of a schema-mismatch error.

inventory.name: auto
inventory.description: Session-start convenience wrapper: checks memory.auto_sync in .loa.config.yaml, and if true, calls sync_notes(); otherwise logs and no-ops. Intended as the one command hooked into automated flows.
inventory.help_probe: not probed (transitively mutates DB when enabled); read from source (auto_sync, memory-sync.sh:313-326).
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: stderr only.
inventory.exit_code_notes: effectively always 0 in the disabled case (return 0 at 321); when enabled, propagates sync_notes' return, which itself frequently returns 0 for no-op cases — the 'auto' entrypoint therefore almost never signals failure via exit code even when underlying add calls are silently failing (per-item failures at memory-sync.sh:220-225 are logged but never escalate the function's own return code).
error_samples: "Auto-sync disabled in config" (memory-sync.sh:320, exit 0 — informational, not an error)
evidence: memory-sync.sh:220-225 per-item add failures are logged but never escalate the caller's return code
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery: per-item memory-admin.sh add failures inside the loop this delegates to (memory-sync.sh:220-225) are caught, logged, and swallowed — `auto` can silently fail to persist every single learning while still exiting 0, exactly the failure mode a session-start hook nobody watches interactively is least equipped to catch.

inventory.name: status
inventory.description: Read-only diagnostic: reports memory-admin DB health + stats, NOTES.md presence + learnings count + last sync time, and trajectory-log file count — the notes-sync-side equivalent of qmd-sync.sh's status.
inventory.help_probe: arguably safe (no mutations in show_status) but excluded from the run to respect the tool's exact --help/-h/help-only constraint; read from source (show_status, memory-sync.sh:332-372).
inventory.json_mode: no — plain-text report, the same gap as qmd-sync.sh's status
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: unlike every other function in this script, show_status's `echo` lines (333-371) go to STDOUT with no log_ wrapper — status is the one memory-sync.sh subcommand meant to be captured/piped, but it's free text, not JSON.
inventory.exit_code_notes: always exits 0 — even 'Memory Database: NOT INITIALIZED' and 'NOTES.md: NOT FOUND' are reported as normal text with no non-zero signal.
worst_suspects: output_parseability: the one subcommand designed for stdout capture in this script has no JSON mode, forcing an agent to regex `Total memories: (\d+)` etc. out of prose — the same self-documentation/parseability gap as qmd-sync.sh's status, suggesting a family-wide pattern rather than a one-off.

evidence.agent_intuitiveness: probe transcript: `timeout 10 bash .claude/scripts/memory-admin.sh --help` exit 0, full usage to stdout (38 lines)
evidence.agent_ergonomics: single --help call surfaces full command+flag+example table (memory-admin.sh:812-853), verified probe
evidence.agent_ease_of_use: same probe: usage lists all 9 subcommands + per-command options + 4 worked examples
evidence.composability: log_info/success/warn/error all `>&2` at memory-admin.sh:58-72; tty-guarded ANSI at :44-52 (verified via Read)
evidence.regression_resistance: test-memory-stack.bats has 24 @test cases covering init/add/list/search/delete/stats/export/import/prune
evidence.self_documentation: cmd_help memory-admin.sh:812-853 lists all commands/options/examples but omits env-var overrides at :24-31

quick_wins: add a consistent --json flag across all subcommands instead of mixed default-JSON/plain-text
document the 4 env-var overrides (LOA_DIR/DB_FILE/EMBED_SCRIPT/CONFIG_FILE) in cmd_help
use distinct exit codes per failure class (not-initialized vs bad-args vs not-found)
Document the 4 env-var overrides (LOA_DIR/DB_FILE/EMBED_SCRIPT/CONFIG_FILE) in cmd_help (memory-admin.sh:812-853)
Give distinct exit codes per failure class instead of blanket exit 1 everywhere so callers can branch on $? not stderr text

inventory.name: post-merge-orchestrator.sh
inventory.description: Top-level pipeline entrypoint (invoked by CI post-merge workflow and manually) that runs classify→semver→version_bump→changelog→gt_regen→rtfm→tag→release→lore_promote→notify phases after a PR merges. Most-touched surface in the family — every other script is either called by it or feeds it.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage block (11 lines) to stdout, no stderr, no ANSI
inventory.json_mode: partial: at the very end `cat "$STATE_FILE"` (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1326) dumps the full post-merge-state.json to stdout, but every phase before that prints free-text `[PHASE] ...` banner/log lines via echo to the SAME stdout stream first — there is no `--json`-only mode, so an agent must locate the JSON object boundary itself rather than pipe straight to `jq`.
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Phase progress (`[CLASSIFY] ...`, `[SEMVER] ...`, banner separators) goes to stdout via echo; `ERROR:`/`WARNING:` lines (missing args, gh unavailable) go to stderr. grep for tput/\033/color vars/tty checks across the whole family returned nothing — no ANSI-into-pipe risk.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Docstring (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:13-16) documents 0/1/2 (2 = 'fatal error: state file corruption, missing dependencies'), but grep shows zero `exit 2` sites anywhere in the script body — that branch is dead documentation. Every phase failure is swallowed by `"phase_${phase}" || true` (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1299), so the script exits 0 even when every single phase failed; only pre-flight arg validation (missing/invalid --pr/--type/--sha) produces the real non-zero exit, and that's always exit 1, never 2.
error_samples: ERROR: --pr is required (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1362)
ERROR: Invalid --type: ${PR_TYPE} (expected cycle|bugfix|other) (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1377)
ERROR: --sha must be a valid git commit hash (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1388)
worst_suspects: determinism_and_reproducibility — exit code is 0 on any structurally-valid invocation regardless of how many phases internally failed; a caller polling exit code alone cannot detect a broken release run, must separately parse `metrics.phases_failed` in the JSON dump
self_documentation — header docstring's 3-way exit contract (0/1/2) is aspirational; exit 2 is never emitted by any code path

inventory.name: --pr NUMBER
inventory.description: Required PR number the orchestrator classifies/tags/releases against; validated as all-numeric after full arg parsing completes (H-04).
inventory.help_probe: shown in --help output: '--pr NUMBER          Source PR number (required)'; help text gives no format constraint (numeric-only) beyond the word 'required'
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Missing/invalid values surface only as a single ERROR line on stderr after the whole arg-parse loop finishes; no early per-flag validation during parsing itself.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 on missing (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1361-1364) or non-numeric (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1380-1384) --pr.
error_samples: ERROR: --pr is required (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1362)
ERROR: --pr must be a number (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1382)
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy — 'ERROR: --pr is required' doesn't restate the expected form or point back at --help; an agent must already know the full flag surface to self-correct

intent_inference — no positional or short-flag fallback (e.g. `-p`); a guessed alternate spelling falls through to the generic 'Unknown argument' path (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1344) rather than a targeted hint

inventory.name: --type TYPE
inventory.description: Required PR-type selector (cycle|bugfix|other) that picks which phase matrix (CYCLE_PHASES / BUGFIX_PHASES / OTHER_PHASES, post-merge-orchestrator.sh:42-44) runs — this is the single value most responsible for the documented known-failure class (misclassification silently downgrades a whole phase set).
inventory.help_probe: shown in --help output: '--type TYPE          PR type: cycle|bugfix|other (required)'; help text does not mention that --type is normally supplied by classify-merge-pr.sh/classify-pr-type.sh rather than hand-typed, nor the release/* branch classification gap
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Enum validation happens only after full arg parse; invalid values print one ERROR line to stderr.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 if missing (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1365-1368) or not one of cycle|bugfix|other (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1375-1378).
error_samples: ERROR: --type is required (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1366)
ERROR: Invalid --type: ${PR_TYPE} (expected cycle|bugfix|other) (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1377)
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery — a syntactically-valid but SEMANTICALLY wrong --type (e.g. 'other' when the merge was really a release cycle) passes this validation silently; the script has no cross-check against the merge subject or labels, it trusts the caller completely — this is exactly the shape of the documented 2026-07-10 release/* misclassification incident

inventory.name: --sha COMMIT
inventory.description: Merge commit SHA; validated as a 7-40 char hex string (H-05). Used to auto-derive --pr from the commit message when classify-driven auto-classification runs, and as the anchor for downstream git log ranges.
inventory.help_probe: shown in --help output: '--sha COMMIT         Merge commit SHA (required)'; accepted length range (7-40 hex chars) is not documented anywhere in --help, only discoverable by reading the regex
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Single ERROR line to stderr on missing or malformed SHA.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 if missing (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1369-1372) or fails the hex regex (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1387-1390).
error_samples: ERROR: --sha is required (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1370)
ERROR: --sha must be a valid git commit hash (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1388)
worst_suspects: agent_ergonomics — error message doesn't state the accepted length range; an agent passing an abbreviated 6-char SHA (common `git log --oneline` output) gets rejected with no hint of the minimum 7-char floor

inventory.name: --dry-run
inventory.description: Validates the pipeline without executing side effects (skips git tag/push, `gh release create`, and file commits). Every phase instead prints a 'Would ...' line and records `dry_run: true` in its state-JSON result.
inventory.help_probe: shown in --help output: '--dry-run            Validate without executing side effects'
inventory.json_mode: partial: the `dry_run: true` marker only exists inside the phase's JSON result object in the state dump; the stdout log line distinguishes real vs. simulated action purely via a trailing '(dry-run)' text suffix, not a structured field
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: '[TAG] Would create tag ${tag} (dry-run)' vs '[TAG] Created and pushed ${tag}' share the identical `[TAG] ` prefix and differ only in trailing text — grepping on the phase tag alone cannot distinguish simulated from real action.
inventory.exit_code_notes: No dedicated exit code for dry-run mode; same 0/1 contract as a live run.
worst_suspects: output_parseability — dry-run and live-run lines share the same `[PHASE] ...` prefix; a caller must string-match the '(dry-run)' suffix rather than check a stable structured flag on stdout to know whether anything actually mutated

inventory.name: --skip-gt / --skip-rtfm / --downstream
inventory.description: --skip-gt / --skip-rtfm bypass the ground-truth-regen and RTFM-validation phases entirely (non-blocking phases per C-MERGE-003). --downstream filters commits to app-zone only for consumer repos; if omitted, the script auto-detects downstream-ness via classify-commit-zone.sh's `is_loa_repo` (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1350-1358).
inventory.help_probe: all three documented tersely in --help output ('Skip ground truth regeneration' / 'Skip RTFM validation' / 'Filter commits to app-zone only (for downstream repos)'); the auto-detection fallback behavior for --downstream is NOT mentioned in --help at all
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Skipped phases print `[GT_REGEN] Skipped via --skip-gt` / `[RTFM] Skipped via --skip-rtfm` to stdout and record status:'skipped' in state JSON — consistent shape.
inventory.exit_code_notes: No dedicated exit codes; these are boolean toggles consumed silently in phase bodies.
evidence: post-merge-orchestrator.sh:1348-1358 (auto-downstream detection, silent unless --downstream explicitly passed)
worst_suspects: self_documentation — the silent auto-detection of downstream mode when --downstream is omitted (via `is_loa_repo`) is undocumented in --help; an agent reading only --help would assume --downstream is purely opt-in when it can actually activate itself

inventory.name: classify-merge-pr.sh
inventory.description: Workflow-level wrapper (Issue #668) that classifies a merged PR into cycle|bugfix|other. PRIMARY signal is the merge commit subject (in-tree, never empty); SECONDARY is `gh pr view` label enrichment, which fails-open to PRIMARY-only with a loud stderr WARN on gh failure. This is the script whose output feeds --type into post-merge-orchestrator.sh.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, 2-line usage synopsis + 4-line description to stdout, no stderr, no ANSI
inventory.json_mode: no: output is `key=value` lines (`pr_type=...`, `pr_number=...`), not JSON — differs from semver-bump.sh's JSON-to-stdout convention elsewhere in the same family
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Result lines (`pr_type=`, `pr_number=`) go to stdout; all WARN/ERROR diagnostics (gh failure, sha-resolution failure) go to stderr prefixed `[classify-merge-pr]` — clean separation, greppable prefix.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Matches its own docstring exactly: 0 = classified successfully, 2 = bad arguments (classify-merge-pr.sh:23-26) — unlike post-merge-orchestrator.sh and release-notes-gen.sh, this script's exit-code contract has no dead/undocumented branches.
error_samples: ERROR: one of --merge-sha or --merge-msg is required (classify-merge-pr.sh:72)
[classify-merge-pr] WARN: gh pr view failed for PR #${PR_NUMBER}; falling through to subject-only classification (classify-merge-pr.sh:118)
evidence: classify-merge-pr.sh:79-85 (git log -1 --format=%s with no `-C "$PROJECT_ROOT"`, unlike siblings)
worst_suspects: composability — this is the only script in the family that does NOT source bootstrap.sh / anchor git via `-C "$PROJECT_ROOT"`; its `git log` call runs against the CLI's current working directory, so invoking it from a subdirectory (a plausible agent mistake, e.g. from inside `.claude/scripts/`) silently changes its answer instead of erroring

inventory.name: --merge-sha <sha>
inventory.description: Resolves the merge commit subject via `git log -1 --format=%s` when --merge-msg is not also given; this is the PRIMARY classification signal.
inventory.help_probe: documented in --help output first usage line: 'classify-merge-pr.sh --merge-sha <sha> [--pr-number <n>]'
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: On resolution failure, falls back to an empty subject with a stderr WARN rather than erroring — an empty subject then classifies as 'other' via classify_pr_type's fallthrough (classify-pr-type.sh:71), a silent-degrade path.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 2 only if the flag's value is missing entirely (classify-merge-pr.sh:52); a bad/non-existent SHA does NOT exit non-zero, it degrades to empty subject and exits 0.
error_samples: ERROR: --merge-sha requires a value (classify-merge-pr.sh:52)
[classify-merge-pr] WARN: failed to resolve subject from sha=$MERGE_SHA (classify-merge-pr.sh:82)
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery — passing a garbage/non-existent SHA is NOT an error; it silently degrades to pr_type=other with exit 0, which is indistinguishable on exit code from a legitimately-classified 'other' PR

inventory.name: --merge-msg "<subject>"
inventory.description: Directly supplies the merge subject, bypassing the `--merge-sha` git lookup. An explicitly-empty string is treated as valid input (classified as 'other') rather than a missing-argument error — MERGE_MSG_SET tracks this distinction.
inventory.help_probe: documented in --help output second usage line: 'classify-merge-pr.sh --merge-msg "<subject>" [--pr-number <n>]'; the empty-string-is-valid nuance is not mentioned in --help text
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: No diagnostic output specific to this flag beyond the shared 'one of --merge-sha or --merge-msg is required' check.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 2 if the flag's value is missing entirely (classify-merge-pr.sh:55); exit 2 if NEITHER --merge-sha nor --merge-msg was passed at all (classify-merge-pr.sh:71-75).
error_samples: ERROR: --merge-msg requires a value (classify-merge-pr.sh:55)
ERROR: one of --merge-sha or --merge-msg is required (classify-merge-pr.sh:72)
worst_suspects: intent_inference — the MERGE_MSG_SET boolean distinguishing 'flag omitted' from 'flag passed empty string' is invisible to a caller; `--merge-msg ""` and omitting the flag entirely look identical in any transcript/log of the invocation

inventory.name: classify_pr_type() rule chain (sourced from classify-pr-type.sh)
inventory.description: The actual verb this script performs: classify_pr_type() (classify-pr-type.sh:42-72, sourced at classify-merge-pr.sh:100) applies 5 precedence rules — label contains 'cycle' → cycle; title matches `\bcycle-[0-9]+\b` → cycle; title starts with Run Mode/Sprint Plan/feat(sprint/feat(cycle) → cycle; title starts with 'fix' → bugfix; else → other. THIS is the root cause of the documented 2026-07-10 known failure: a `release/*` branch merge subject (e.g. 'Merge pull request #1201 from org/release/v1.196.0') matches none of the cycle rules and doesn't start with 'fix', so it falls through to 'other' — there is no rule for release/* branches anywhere in the chain.
inventory.help_probe: N/A — not a CLI flag; this is internal logic invoked implicitly after arg parsing, so --help (which only documents the two mutually-exclusive input flags) says nothing about the classification rules or their release/* gap. Confirmed via source read only, not a runnable probe.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: The classification result is silent — no intermediate reasoning is logged to stdout or stderr about WHY a title was classified a given way, only the final `pr_type=` line. An agent debugging a misclassification has no trace of which of the 5 rules fired (or didn't).
inventory.exit_code_notes: Always returns via `echo` + implicit 0; classify_pr_type() never itself signals failure — 'other' is treated as a legitimate, first-class classification outcome, not an error.
evidence: classify-pr-type.sh:30-41 (5-rule precedence comment, no release/* rule)
classify-pr-type.sh:56 (`\bcycle-[0-9]+\b` — a release/vX.Y.Z branch name does not match)
classify-pr-type.sh:66 (`^fix` bugfix rule — release merges don't start with fix)
worst_suspects: intent_inference — the rule set has zero awareness of branch-name conventions (release/*, hotfix/*); it only inspects the merge commit SUBJECT text, so any branch-naming convention that doesn't also encode 'cycle'/'fix' in the subject text silently falls to 'other'

error_pedagogy — there is no warning, log line, or exit-code signal anywhere when a merge falls through all 4 positive rules to 'other'; the miss is completely silent from the CLI surface, only discoverable by manually reading pr_type= after the fact

inventory.name: GITHUB_OUTPUT
inventory.description: When set (GitHub Actions runner convention), the same `pr_type=`/`pr_number=` lines are ALSO appended to the file at $GITHUB_OUTPUT, in addition to stdout — a dual-channel output.
inventory.help_probe: not mentioned anywhere in --help output (usage(), classify-merge-pr.sh:37-47) — an agent reading only --help would not learn this side-channel exists
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Silent append (`>>`) to a file path taken from environment with no existence/writability check beforehand — if $GITHUB_OUTPUT is set but points at a bad path, the `>>` redirection failure is NOT caught (no `|| true`/error check around it), which under a caller's `set -e` wrapper could abort the invoking shell.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Script itself always exit 0 after this block (line 147) regardless of whether the GITHUB_OUTPUT write succeeded.
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery — the append to $GITHUB_OUTPUT has no error handling; a stale/invalid path in that env var fails silently from this script's perspective (still exits 0) while potentially propagating a different failure upstream depending on the caller's shell options

inventory.name: semver-bump.sh
inventory.description: Conventional-commit semver calculator. Reads the current version (from git tag or CHANGELOG header) and the commits since it, then computes the next bump. This is the script whose default behavior (patch-bump when no feat:/fix: commits exist) is the proximate mechanical cause of the 2026-07-10 known failure once classify-merge-pr.sh has already mis-typed a release/* merge as 'other'.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage + options block (6 lines) to stdout, no stderr, no ANSI
inventory.json_mode: yes: always emits a single JSON object `{current, next, bump, commits}` to stdout (semver-bump.sh:11-12, 423-428) — this is the default and only output mode, not a flag-gated one
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Final JSON goes cleanly to stdout via `jq -n ... ` (line 423); ALL diagnostics (ERROR lines) go to stderr. Internally, parse_commits() also emits an intermediate commits-JSON array to file descriptor 3 (semver-bump.sh:186, 254) which is redirected to a tempfile rather than mixed into stdout/stderr — a slightly unusual but clean 3-stream design that keeps final stdout JSON-only and parseable.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Docstring (semver-bump.sh:14-17) says 0=success, 1=no commits since last tag, 2=no version source found — and this DOES match the code exactly (unlike post-merge-orchestrator.sh/release-notes-gen.sh): exit 2 fires on unknown CLI arg (line 282), missing tags in --from-tag mode (292), missing/unmatched CHANGELOG version (296, 301-302), and no-version-source in auto mode (312-313, 316-317); exit 1 fires on zero commits since tag (326-327) and on the downstream-filter-removed-everything case (404-407, not mentioned in the docstring at all).
error_samples: ERROR: No version tags found (semver-bump.sh:292)
ERROR: No tag matching v${current} found (semver-bump.sh:301)
ERROR: No commits since ${tag_ref} (semver-bump.sh:326)
evidence: semver-bump.sh:404-407 (undocumented 3rd exit-1 cause: all commits filtered as internal under --downstream)
worst_suspects: self_documentation — the docstring's exit-1 reason list ('no commits since last tag') omits the second real exit-1 cause introduced later (--downstream filtering removes every commit, line 404-407); the contract table wasn't updated when that branch was added

inventory.name: --from-tag
inventory.description: Forces the current-version source to the latest matching git tag (release or prerelease shape) instead of the default auto-detection.
inventory.help_probe: documented in --help output: 'Usage: semver-bump.sh [--from-tag | --from-changelog] [--downstream]' — the mutual-exclusivity implied by the `|` is not enforced in code (passing both silently lets the later flag win, last-write-wins on `source_mode`)
inventory.json_mode: yes (same JSON-to-stdout as script default)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a beyond script-level notes
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 2 if no tags matching the SemVer regex exist at all (semver-bump.sh:292).
error_samples: ERROR: No version tags found (semver-bump.sh:292)
worst_suspects: regression_resistance — `--from-tag --from-changelog` together is silently accepted (last one wins) despite the usage synopsis implying `|` (mutual exclusivity); no validation rejects the combination

inventory.name: --from-changelog
inventory.description: Forces the current-version source to the CHANGELOG.md `## [X.Y.Z]` header, then requires a matching git tag `vX.Y.Z` to exist as the diff anchor — this is the specific path named in the task's known-failure callout.
inventory.help_probe: documented in --help output alongside --from-tag on the same synopsis line
inventory.json_mode: yes (same JSON-to-stdout as script default)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a beyond script-level notes
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 2 if CHANGELOG.md has no `## [X.Y.Z]` header at all (semver-bump.sh:296), OR if the CHANGELOG's version has no matching git tag (semver-bump.sh:298-303) — this second case is the exact scenario in the task's callout: a version documented in CHANGELOG but never tagged (e.g. after a misclassified/skipped tag phase) makes every subsequent `--from-changelog` invocation hard-fail rather than fall back.
error_samples: ERROR: No version in CHANGELOG.md (semver-bump.sh:296)
ERROR: No tag matching v${current} found (semver-bump.sh:301)
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy — 'No tag matching v${current} found' doesn't say WHERE current came from (CHANGELOG.md header) or suggest the fix (create the missing tag, or fall back to `--from-tag`/auto); an agent seeing only this line has to read the source to understand the two-step CHANGELOG→tag dependency

inventory.name: --downstream
inventory.description: Filters the commit list to app-zone only (via classify-commit-zone.sh) before computing the bump, so downstream/consumer repos don't count framework-internal commits toward their own release version.
inventory.help_probe: documented in --help output: '--downstream  Filter out non-app commits (system-only, state-only, mixed-internal)'
inventory.json_mode: yes (same JSON-to-stdout, but `commits` array and `bump` reflect the filtered set)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Silently no-ops (keeps unfiltered results) if classify-commit-zone.sh isn't found at the expected path (semver-bump.sh:356 `if [[ -f "$classify_script" ]]`) — no warning printed when the filter is skipped due to a missing helper.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 if the filter removes every single commit (semver-bump.sh:404-407) — this exit-1 cause is NOT listed in the script's own top-of-file exit-code docstring.
error_samples: ERROR: No app-zone commits since ${tag_ref} (all filtered as internal) (semver-bump.sh:405)
worst_suspects: self_documentation — silent fallback when classify-commit-zone.sh is missing means `--downstream` can quietly behave as a no-op with zero signal that filtering didn't happen

inventory.description: The most load-bearing exit-code contract in the family for the known-failure narrative — this is the ONE script whose documented dictionary is (nearly) accurate, which matters because post-merge-orchestrator.sh's phase_semver treats any non-zero exit as an undifferentiated 'semver calculation failed' (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:307-313), collapsing the 1-vs-2 distinction anyway.
inventory.help_probe: not reiterated in --help output itself (--help only shows usage/options, not the exit-code table — that's header-comment-only documentation, invisible to `--help` callers)
inventory.json_mode: no (exit codes are never surfaced in the JSON payload; a failure means NO json is emitted at all, just a stderr line)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: On any exit 1/2, nothing goes to stdout (the jq JSON build at the end never runs) — a caller must not assume 'stdout empty' means 'nothing happened', it specifically means the whole calculation aborted.
inventory.exit_code_notes: 0 = success (JSON emitted); 1 = no commits since anchor tag (326-327) or all commits downstream-filtered away (404-407, undocumented); 2 = bad CLI arg (282), missing version source in any mode (292, 296, 301-302, 312-313, 316-317).
error_samples: ERROR: Unknown argument: $1 (semver-bump.sh:282)
ERROR: No commits since ${tag_ref} (semver-bump.sh:326)
evidence: post-merge-orchestrator.sh:298-313 (caller collapses 1 vs 2 into one generic failure message)
worst_suspects: output_parseability — because post-merge-orchestrator.sh's phase_semver captures the result with `2>/dev/null` (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:299) before checking success, the ACTUAL semver-bump.sh stderr message (which distinguishes the 6+ distinct failure causes) is thrown away by the caller and replaced with the single generic '[SEMVER] Failed — semver calculation error' — all of semver-bump.sh's careful per-cause error messages never reach the orchestrator's own log or state JSON

inventory.name: highest_bump="patch" default (no error emitted — silent behavior)
inventory.description: parse_commits() initializes `highest_bump="patch"` (semver-bump.sh:190) before scanning commits; if none of the commits since the anchor tag match a recognized conventional-commit type (feat/fix/perf/refactor/chore/docs/test/ci/style/build), the bump silently stays at the initial 'patch' value with zero warning. Combined with post-merge-orchestrator.sh's phase_semver NEVER passing `--from-tag`/`--from-changelog` (post-merge-orchestrator.sh:293-296 only conditionally adds `--downstream`), production always runs in 'auto' source mode. This is the second half of the documented 2026-07-10 mechanism: once classify-merge-pr.sh mis-typed a release/* merge as 'other' (see classify-merge-pr.sh::subcommand::classification-logic), semver-bump.sh's default-to-patch behavior is what actually produced the wrong tag.
inventory.help_probe: N/A — not an emitted error message at all; this is a silent default, discoverable only by reading source. --help gives no hint that 'no recognized commit types' silently resolves to patch rather than erroring or warning.
inventory.json_mode: yes — the resulting `{"bump": "patch", ...}` JSON is emitted looking exactly as confident/successful as any correctly-computed bump; there is no `confidence` or `matched_commits` field distinguishing 'we found real feat:/fix: commits' from 'nothing matched, this is just the fallback default'.
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: No warning printed anywhere (stdout or stderr) when zero commits matched a known conventional-commit prefix — completely silent degrade-to-default.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 0 — a 'nothing recognized, defaulted to patch' run is indistinguishable by exit code from a 'genuinely detected a patch-level fix: commit' run.
evidence: semver-bump.sh:227-231 (unrecognized commit type falls to `commit_bump="patch"` via `${BUMP_MAP[$type]:-}` empty check)
post-merge-orchestrator.sh:293-296 (semver_args never includes --from-tag/--from-changelog; always auto mode in production)
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy — this is the single biggest gap in the whole family: the exact mechanism of the documented real-world incident (release/* merge, no feat: commits → silent patch bump) produces ZERO error, warning, or degraded-confidence signal anywhere in stdout, stderr, or the JSON payload; the only way to have caught it pre-merge was reading source

regression_resistance — nothing in the JSON schema (`current/next/bump/commits`) distinguishes 'bump computed from N matched conventional commits' vs 'bump defaulted because 0 commits matched any known type' — a trivial `matched_commit_count` field would have made this mechanically detectable

inventory.name: release-notes-gen.sh
inventory.description: Generates release notes markdown for a given version via a 3-tier fallback: (1) extract the matching CHANGELOG.md section, (2) synthesize from PR title/body via `gh pr view`, (3) compile raw commit log. Called by post-merge-orchestrator.sh's phase_release to build the `gh release create --notes` body.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage + options block (6 lines) to stdout, no stderr, no ANSI
inventory.json_mode: no: output is Markdown to stdout by design (release-notes-gen.sh:11)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: All generated notes content goes to stdout; the only stderr traffic is the two required-arg ERROR lines and whatever `gh`/`git` commands emit that isn't already redirected to /dev/null (most are).
inventory.exit_code_notes: Docstring (release-notes-gen.sh:14-16) documents 0/1/2 (2 = 'CHANGELOG not found or version not found'), but — same pattern as post-merge-orchestrator.sh — grep confirms there is NO `exit 2` anywhere in the actual script body. The 3-tier fallback design means extract_changelog_section() failing (missing CHANGELOG or version) never surfaces as an error at all: it just falls through to Tier 2 (PR metadata) or Tier 3 (commit log, which itself has a further fallback to a bare 'Release v${version}.' line at release-notes-gen.sh:241 if even git log is empty) — the script is architected to ALWAYS succeed with exit 0, making the documented exit 2 permanently unreachable.
error_samples: ERROR: --version is required (release-notes-gen.sh:367)
ERROR: --pr is required (release-notes-gen.sh:372)
evidence: release-notes-gen.sh:240-243 (Tier 3's own final fallback to a 1-line stub — the fallback of the fallback)
worst_suspects: self_documentation — identical defect shape to post-merge-orchestrator.sh: exit code 2 is documented in the header comment but the multi-tier fallback design means it is structurally unreachable in the actual implementation

output_parseability — a caller cannot distinguish 'rich CHANGELOG-sourced notes' from 'nothing matched anywhere, here is a bare `Release vX.Y.Z.` stub' by exit code or any structured signal — both return exit 0 with Markdown on stdout; only the CONTENT differs

inventory.name: --version VERSION / --pr NUMBER
inventory.description: Both required together: --version selects the CHANGELOG section / tag range to extract from; --pr is used for PR-metadata synthesis (Tier 2) and is always printed verbatim in the '### Source' footer regardless of which tier produced the body.
inventory.help_probe: documented in --help output: '--version VERSION    Version to generate notes for (required)' / '--pr NUMBER          Source PR number (required)'
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Missing-arg checks run sequentially (version checked before pr) so only ONE of the two ERROR lines is ever shown per invocation even if both are missing — an agent omitting both flags sees only the --version error, fixes it, reruns, and only THEN discovers --pr is also missing (release-notes-gen.sh:366-373).
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 for either missing required flag (release-notes-gen.sh:368, 373) — no --pr format validation (unlike post-merge-orchestrator.sh's numeric-only check on the same conceptual value).
error_samples: ERROR: --version is required (release-notes-gen.sh:367)
ERROR: --pr is required (release-notes-gen.sh:372)
worst_suspects: agent_ergonomics — sequential (not batched) required-arg validation means an agent missing multiple flags gets corrected one-at-a-time across multiple failed invocations instead of a single 'both --version and --pr are required' message

inventory.name: --type TYPE
inventory.description: Optional PR-type selector (cycle|bugfix|other, default 'other' per release-notes-gen.sh:29) that picks which of the three near-identical template functions (generate_cycle_notes / generate_bugfix_notes / generate_other_notes) runs.
inventory.help_probe: documented in --help output: '--type TYPE          PR type: cycle|bugfix|other (default: other)'
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: No validation at all on the value — an unrecognized string (e.g. a typo like 'cyle') silently falls through the `case` statement's `other|*)` catch-all (release-notes-gen.sh:379) and is treated as 'other' with zero warning, identical to passing the flag correctly.
inventory.exit_code_notes: No dedicated exit code; unrecognized --type values never error, matching the family-wide pattern of typos being silently absorbed into the default branch rather than rejected.
evidence: release-notes-gen.sh:376-380 (`case "$PR_TYPE" in cycle) ...; bugfix) ...; other|*) ...; esac` — no rejection of unknown values)
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery — unlike post-merge-orchestrator.sh (which DOES reject an invalid --type with exit 1), this script's --type accepts ANY string silently as 'other' via the `*)` catch-all — a typo'd type value from a caller script produces wrong-but-plausible notes with no signal anything was misconfigured

inventory.name: 0 / 1 / 2 (documented) — 0 / 1 (actual)
inventory.description: Family-wide pattern check: this is the third of four scripts (alongside post-merge-orchestrator.sh) whose header docstring promises an exit-2 fatal-error path that the implementation never actually takes — only classify-merge-pr.sh and semver-bump.sh have accurate exit-code contracts in this family.
inventory.help_probe: not reiterated in --help output (exit codes are header-comment-only, invisible to `--help` callers across the whole family)
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a beyond script-level notes above
inventory.exit_code_notes: Actual behavior: 0 = notes generated (always, via 3-tier fallback that bottoms out at a guaranteed-non-empty stub); 1 = missing --version or --pr. The documented '2 = CHANGELOG not found or version not found' never fires — grep of the whole file confirms zero `exit 2` occurrences.

ERROR: --pr is required (release-notes-gen.sh:372)

inventory.name: validate-skill-capabilities.sh
inventory.description: MUST-gate linter over every .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md frontmatter: capabilities/cost-profile presence, Flatline SKP-003 'all' sentinel ban, execute_commands grammar, capabilities-vs-allowed-tools consistency, cycle-108 role/primary_role rules, cycle-119 C13 model:/agent: bans on role:review|audit, and the agent-type-vs-write-capability invariant (issue #553). Agents run it after touching any SKILL.md and in CI.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage text to stdout, 4 lines. Identical for both --help and -h (same case branch, lines 37-43).
inventory.json_mode: yes: --json (jq -n object {total,passed,errors,warnings,strict,results:[{skill,level,message}]}, lines 645-654). All human echo lines are gated on JSON_OUTPUT==false first, so stdout is pure JSON in --json mode.
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Human-mode PASS/WARN/ERROR lines go to stdout via `echo -e` with hardcoded ANSI codes (RED/GREEN/YELLOW, lines 312-315) and there is NO tty/isatty guard anywhere in the file (confirmed by grep across all three family scripts) — colors are emitted unconditionally into pipes/log files whenever --json is not passed. The one true usage-error message ('Unknown arg') goes to stderr (line 44).
inventory.exit_code_notes: 0 = clean; 1 = errors>0 (includes --strict-promoted warnings, line 656-658); 2 = usage error (unknown flag, line 44) or --skill NAME not found (line 630-631). No exit-code table appears anywhere in the --help output (lines 38-43) — an agent must read source to learn code 2 exists, or that 1 vs 2 mean different classes of problem.
error_samples: validate-skill-capabilities.sh:44: "Unknown arg: $1" (stderr, exit 2)
validate-skill-capabilities.sh:630: "Skill not found: $SINGLE_SKILL" (stderr, exit 2)
worst_suspects: self_documentation: --help text (lines 38-43) never mentions the 0/1/2 exit-code contract or the error-vs-warning severity split
output_parseability: ANSI color codes (lines 312-315) are written unconditionally whenever --json is absent, with zero tty detection anywhere in the file

inventory.name: --json
inventory.description: Switches all per-skill PASS/WARN/ERROR echoes off and emits a single jq-built result object on stdout at end of run (line 645-654). Used by agents/CI that need to programmatically enumerate which skills failed and why.
inventory.help_probe: not independently probed (execution restricted to --help/-h/help); flag behavior confirmed from source read only
inventory.json_mode: yes: this IS the JSON-mode switch. Schema: {total:int, passed:int, errors:int, warnings:int, strict:bool, results:[{skill:string, level:'error'|'warning', message:string}]}
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A directly; see script-level surface for the color/tty note this flag exists to work around.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Does not change the exit-code contract — --json only changes what appears on stdout, the exit code logic (lines 656-659) is identical with or without it.
worst_suspects: self_documentation: help text's one-line description ('--json  Output as JSON', line 40) never states the schema shape — an agent has to run it once and reverse-engineer field names, or read the script

inventory.name: --strict
inventory.description: Promotes every WARN to an ERROR (log_warning calls log_error and returns 1 to the caller, lines 326-337), used in CI to fail the build on soft findings that are advisory in local dev.
inventory.help_probe: not independently probed (execution restricted to --help/-h/help); flag behavior confirmed from source read only
inventory.json_mode: partial: --strict changes which entries land in the JSON 'errors' vs 'warnings' bucket but the promoted message text gets ' (strict: promoted from warning)' appended (line 329) so the two code paths are distinguishable in JSON output.
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: No independent stream behavior; reuses log_error's stdout echo (non-JSON mode) / results_json entry (JSON mode).
inventory.exit_code_notes: Indirect: promoting warnings to errors can flip the final exit code from 0 to 1 for a run that would otherwise pass (errors-count now includes what were warnings).
error_samples: validate-skill-capabilities.sh:329: "$msg (strict: promoted from warning)"
worst_suspects: agent_ergonomics: all-or-nothing — there is no way to promote only a subset of warning classes (e.g. only agent-type invariant warnings) to hard failures; an agent tuning CI strictness has one binary knob

inventory.name: --skill NAME
inventory.description: Restricts the run to a single skill directory under SKILLS_DIR instead of scanning all of .claude/skills/*. Used by an agent that just edited one SKILL.md and wants a fast targeted check.
inventory.help_probe: not independently probed (execution restricted to --help/-h/help); flag behavior confirmed from source read only
inventory.json_mode: yes, inherits --json behavior for the single-skill run.
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: The 'not found' error is the one path in this script that writes to stderr with a bare, unstructured string even when --json was requested (line 630 runs before any JSON emission logic and is unconditional).
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 2 if the named directory doesn't exist under SKILLS_DIR (lines 627-631); otherwise same 0/1 contract as a normal run.

inventory.name: -h / --help

inventory.exit_code_notes: -h/--help: exit 0. Bare 'help' positional: exit 2 (same as any other unrecognized token).

worst_suspects: intent_inference: an agent that tries the bare word 'help' (a common LLM instinct, mirroring many CLIs' subcommand-style help) is silently treated as an invalid flag rather than routed to usage text

inventory.name: 0/1/2 exit contract
inventory.description: 0 = every checked skill passed (or --strict didn't promote anything); 1 = at least one ERROR-level finding across all checked skills; 2 = the invocation itself was malformed (unknown flag, or --skill named a nonexistent directory).
inventory.help_probe: N/A (not a flag)
inventory.json_mode: yes: the JSON object's top-level `errors` count is what actually drives the eventual exit 1 (line 656), so an agent can derive the same signal from JSON without checking $?
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A
inventory.exit_code_notes: Undocumented in --help. 1 and 2 are semantically distinct ('found problems in valid input' vs 'the invocation was wrong') but nothing in the tool's own output ever states this — an agent branching purely on $? cannot tell 'my SKILL.md is broken' (1) apart from 'I typo'd a flag or skill name' (2) without also reading stderr text.
worst_suspects: determinism_and_reproducibility: exit code 2 is reused for two semantically different failure classes (malformed CLI vs. valid CLI/missing target) with no distinguishing exit code between them

inventory.name: capabilities: all sentinel prohibited
inventory.description: Fires when a SKILL.md's frontmatter sets `capabilities: all` instead of an explicit expanded capability map (Flatline finding SKP-003). This is the deny-all-by-default security boundary for the whole skill system.
inventory.help_probe: N/A (not a flag)
inventory.json_mode: yes: appears as a results[] entry with level:'error' in --json mode
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: stdout (non-JSON mode) via log_error, prefixed 'ERROR:' in red.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Always sets has_error=true, contributing to the final exit 1.

inventory.help_probe: N/A (not a flag)

inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: stdout (non-JSON mode) via log_error.

inventory.name: 0/1/2 exit contract (documented vs actual)
inventory.description: Documented (header lines 20-25, --help lines 51-56): 0=consistent, 1=inconsistent OR usage error OR missing-trailer-with-require-trailer, 2=no trailer (legacy pass). Actual code: 0=consistent (line 224); 1=inconsistent trailer content found (line 228) OR missing-trailer-with-require-trailer (line 136); 2=EVERY usage error (missing --file/--gate line 75, invalid --gate line 80, file-not-found line 85, unknown option line 68) AND the legacy-no-trailer-pass case (line 140).
inventory.help_probe: N/A (not a flag)

inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A
inventory.exit_code_notes: CONFIRMED via source: the documented contract states usage errors map to exit 1, but every usage-error branch in the actual case-statement and validation logic exits 2 instead — the same code (2) is documented ONLY as meaning 'legacy file, no trailer, treat as pass' (a benign/success-adjacent signal), yet in practice 2 is ALSO what a malformed `--gate` value or a missing required flag produces. An orchestrator trusting the documented table would treat a usage error (2) as a benign legacy-pass.

worst_suspects: self_documentation: this is a genuine documentation/implementation contradiction, not a style nit — the script's own printed --help text describes an exit-code contract that its own case-statement does not implement

determinism_and_reproducibility: exit code 2 is overloaded to mean both 'benign legacy pass, no action needed' and 'you invoked me wrong' — a caller cannot safely treat 2 as either uniformly good or uniformly bad
