inventory.name: FR-2-AMBIGUOUS rm -rf block message
inventory.description: SEED FINDING S-1. Fires when an `rm -rf` operand falls through the FR-2 per-arg ladder to the 'else' branch (unrecognized shape). Message promises './path/ explicit form' as an escape hatch, but the ALLOW-list regex that decides whether an explicit './x/' form is actually accepted has a mandatory first-character exclusion that silently defeats that promise for the single most common explicit-form target an agent reaches for: any dot-prefixed relative dir/file (`.venv`, `.cache`, `.turbo`, `.pytest_cache`, `.parcel-cache`, ...) OTHER than the three hardcoded exceptions `.git`/`.ssh`/`.env`.
inventory.help_probe: probe skipped: this is a decision branch inside the hook's per-request logic, not a standalone invocable surface — reproducing it requires feeding tool_input.command JSON on stdin, which is a live PreToolUse-hook simulation, out of scope for a --help/-h/help probe under the hard safety rules for this family.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Message goes to stderr only via emit_block (line 348: `echo "BLOCKED [$pattern_id]: $message" >&2`). No ANSI/color codes anywhere in the file (grep for tput/\033/color returned zero hits) so it is pipe-safe, but there is also no stdout companion — an agent piping only stdout sees nothing and must know to check stderr/exit-code 2.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 2 always (via emit_block, line 391). Same exit code (2) as FR-2-BLOCK and every other pattern in the file — the exit code alone never distinguishes 'catastrophic, permanently refused' from 'ambiguous, rephrase and retry'; only the bracketed pattern_id string in the stderr text distinguishes them, and nothing machine-parseable (no JSON, no separate exit code) carries that distinction.
error_samples: BLOCKED [FR-2-AMBIGUOUS]: rm -rf on an unclear path './.venv/'. Use './path/' explicit form, 'trash', or remove targeted children.
BLOCKED [FR-2-AMBIGUOUS]: rm -rf on an unclear path '../build'. Use './path/' explicit form, 'trash', or remove targeted children.
evidence: .claude/hooks/safety/block-destructive-bash.sh:1031 — the exact message text: `emit_block "FR-2-AMBIGUOUS" "$matched_arg" "rm -rf on an unclear path '$matched_arg'. Use './path/' explicit form, 'trash', or remove targeted children."`

.claude/hooks/safety/block-destructive-bash.sh:847 — `_re_allow_list='^(\./[^/*.][^*]*|node_modules$|...)'` — the FIRST character after `./` is matched by `[^/*.]`, a class that explicitly EXCLUDES `.` — so `./.anything` can never match this branch of the allow list, no matter how 'explicit' the `./` prefix is.

.claude/hooks/safety/block-destructive-bash.sh:846 — `_re_allow_exclude='^\./($|\*|\.|\.git$|\.git/|\.ssh$|\.ssh/|\.env)'` — the ONLY dot-prefixed shapes carved out at all are bare `.`, `.git`, `.ssh`, `.env`; every other dotdir (`.venv`, `.cache`, `.turbo`, `.next` is even excluded from allow_list too since `.next` requires no leading `./`) falls through both allow_exclude and allow_list.

.claude/hooks/safety/block-destructive-bash.sh:1000-1021 — the classification ladder: dotdot escape → ambiguous (1000-1002), catastrophic block_list → block (1009-1011), allow_exclude → ambiguous (1013-1015), allow_list → allow/continue (1017-1019), else → ambiguous (1021). A dot-prefixed `./` path (not `.git`/`.ssh`/`.env`) matches none of the first four and always lands on line 1021's unconditional ambiguous fallback — deterministically, on every retry, regardless of how the agent rephrases the `./` prefix.

.claude/hooks/safety/block-destructive-bash.sh:818 — the whole FR-2 ladder (and hence this message) only ever runs when `"$command" == *"rm"*` — confirming the seed finding's reported workaround (`find <path> -delete`) is a real, unguarded escape: it contains no literal `rm` token so it never reaches this code path at all (grep of the file for a `-delete`/find-delete guard, distinct from the unrelated SQL `DELETE FROM` P10 pattern and the find-exec placeholder logic at lines 849-921, returns nothing).
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: the message states a fix ('use ./path/ explicit form') that is provably insufficient for an entire common class of targets (dotdirs), so an agent that follows the advice literally gets blocked again with the identical message — a dead-end masquerading as guidance

intent_inference: the matcher's real criterion is 'does the FIRST character after ./ avoid being a dot', which has zero semantic connection to 'explicit vs ambiguous' as a human or agent would read the term

safety_with_recovery: the only working recovery path an agent is likely to discover by trial-and-error is `find <path> -delete`, which bypasses the fence ENTIRELY rather than satisfying it — the safety net actively pushes agents toward the one shape it cannot see

inventory.name: FR-2 gate trigger condition (rm substring pre-filter)
inventory.description: The literal-substring pre-filter that decides whether the entire FR-2 rm -rf ladder (BLOCK/AMBIGUOUS/allow) evaluates at all. Documents the complementary half of S-1: any recursive-delete-equivalent command that does not contain the two-character literal substring 'rm' (e.g. `find <path> -delete`, `find <path> -exec unlink {} \;`, `python3 -c 'import shutil; shutil.rmtree(...)'`) skips the entire hook family unconditionally — not conservatively, not with a warning, just silently allowed.
inventory.help_probe: probe skipped: gate condition is evaluated per Bash tool_input.command payload, not exposed as an invocable flag/subcommand.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a — this is a control-flow gate, not itself an output-producing surface. When the gate is FALSE the whole hook exits silently with no stdout/stderr at all (falls to line 1037's bare `exit 0`).
inventory.exit_code_notes: When gate is false: exit 0, indistinguishable at the exit-code level from 'every rm arg was on the allow list' — both are silent exit-0 with zero stderr, so an audit log reader cannot tell 'fence evaluated and passed' from 'fence never ran'.
evidence: .claude/hooks/safety/block-destructive-bash.sh:818 — `if [[ "$command" == *"rm"* && "$command" == *"-"* ]] \` — the entire FR-2 block is skipped unless the raw command string contains the literal substrings `rm` and `-`.

.claude/hooks/safety/block-destructive-bash.sh:815-816 (comment) — 'pass-8 pre-filter: `rm` is a mandatory concatenation element of the gate ERE' — confirms this is an intentional performance optimization, not an oversight, but its blast radius (any non-`rm`-named deletion tool) is undocumented in the header's 'accepted bypass classes' list (lines 36-41), which enumerates newline separators, subshells, eval/base64, SQL-comment WHERE, and missing jq — NOT alternate deletion tools.
worst_suspects: regression_resistance: the header's explicit 'accepted bypass classes' documentation (lines 36-41) omits this class entirely, so a future contributor auditing the file for known gaps will not find 'non-rm deletion tools' listed and may assume the fence is broader than it is
self_documentation: the gate's necessity-proof comment justifies the performance shortcut but never states the corollary that any non-'rm' spelled deletion command sails through with zero scrutiny

error_samples: BLOCKED [FR-2-BLOCK]: rm -rf on catastrophic path '/' (system root / home / glob / current-dir) refused. Use 'trash <path>' for a recoverable delete, or an explicit './<path>/' form scoped to a real subdirectory.
evidence: .claude/hooks/safety/block-destructive-bash.sh:845 — `_re_block_list='^(/|\$HOME|\$\{HOME\}|~|~/|/etc|/usr|/var|/home|\*|\.)$|^(/etc/|/usr/|/var/|/home/|~/|\$HOME/$|\$\{HOME\}/$)'` — the exact catastrophic-path set this message is guarding.

inventory.name: block-destructive-bash.sh (whole-script surface)
inventory.description: PreToolUse:Bash safety hook, wired via .claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh, registered against every Bash tool call the agent makes. Not a CLI an agent invokes directly with flags — its sole 'API' is (1) stdin JSON `{tool_input: {command: ...}}` and (2) a small set of env vars. An agent never runs this script by choice; it experiences it only as a side effect of issuing a Bash command.
inventory.help_probe: probe run: `timeout 10 bash .claude/hooks/safety/block-destructive-bash.sh --help` → exit 0, ZERO stdout/stderr output. The script never inspects argv at all; it re-execs under bash>=4 if needed (line 116, preserving argv), checks jq (line 123), then reads stdin via jq (line 141). With no JSON piped in, jq's parse error is redirected to /dev/null (line 141) and `command` resolves empty, hitting the fail-open `exit 0` at line 145 — completely silent, indistinguishable from 'your command was approved'.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Diagnostics (BLOCKED / WARNING) always go to stderr, never stdout; stdout is NEVER written to by this script under any code path (confirmed: no `echo`/`printf` targets stdout anywhere in the file except inside the log-redactor subprocess pipeline which itself writes to a variable, not the hook's own stdout). No ANSI/color codes anywhere (verified via grep for tput/\033/color — zero hits), so no tty-vs-pipe rendering issues.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Two-value contract stated in the header (line 6): 'Exit 0 = allow, Exit 2 = block.' In practice there is a THIRD implicit case not mentioned in that one-line summary — exit 2 for 'the fence itself could not run' (bash<4, line 120) — sharing the same exit code as an actual detected-destructive-command block, distinguishable only by reading the stderr text.

inventory.name: hook-guard.sh (whole-script surface)
inventory.description: Thin wrapper registered as the actual PreToolUse command in settings.hooks.json; it syntax-checks the target hook (e.g. block-destructive-bash.sh) via `bash -n` before executing it, so a git-conflict-markered or otherwise unparseable hook fails OPEN instead of bricking every Bash/Edit call. Usage: `.claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh .claude/hooks/safety/<hook>.sh [args...]`.
inventory.help_probe: probe run: `timeout 10 bash .claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh --help` → exit 0. Because the script naively takes `$1` as the target path with NO flag parsing (line 43), passing '--help' makes `target="--help"`, and the subsequent `bash -n "$target"` (line 46) is executed as `bash -n --help` — bash itself interprets this as an (invalid) top-level CLI invocation and dumps its own multi-line GNU-bash usage banner to STDOUT before hook-guard's own WARN prints to stderr and the script exits 0. Confusing double-banner output for a simple --help request; captured verbatim in error_samples.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: The bash-usage-banner leak goes to STDOUT (bash's own usage text), while hook-guard's own diagnostic goes to stderr — an agent piping only stderr for 'error messages' would miss the (arguably more confusing) stdout banner entirely.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Exits 0 on EITHER parse-fail (line 48, deliberate fail-open) or successful `exec` handoff (line 51, in which case the exit code becomes whatever the wrapped hook exits — 0 or 2 for block-destructive-bash.sh). This means 'hook-guard.sh exited 2' is IMPOSSIBLE by its own logic — any exit 2 an agent observes came from the wrapped hook, never from the guard itself, which is undocumented as an invariant anywhere outward-facing (only inferable from source).
error_samples:
/usr/bin/bash: --: invalid option
Usage:	/usr/bin/bash [GNU long option] [option] ...
	/usr/bin/bash [GNU long option] [option] script-file ...
[... full GNU bash usage banner ...]
[hook-guard] WARN: PreToolUse hook --help failed to parse (bash -n) — failing OPEN (allowing tool); repair the hook, retrying will not help
evidence: .claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh:43-51 — full script body: naive `target="$1"`, `bash -n "$target"` check, WARN+exit0 on failure, else `exec`.
.claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh:6-7 (comment) — documented usage form assumes the FIRST positional arg is always a real hook script path; no defensive handling for a flag-shaped or missing $1.
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: the --help probe produces bash's own confusing multi-line usage dump interleaved with hook-guard's unrelated WARN about a hook 'named --help failing to parse' — actively misleading if an agent is trying to learn how to use this wrapper
self_documentation: no --help of its own; the only usage documentation is the header comment (lines 6-7), unreachable from the CLI itself

inventory.name: hook-guard parse-failure WARN
inventory.description: The ONE diagnostic message this wrapper ever emits — fires whenever `bash -n "$target"` returns non-zero, whether because the target file has a genuine syntax error (conflict markers, unbalanced quotes) OR because $target isn't a real file path at all (as demonstrated by the --help probe).
inventory.help_probe: see hook-guard.sh::script::main — this message was directly captured by that probe.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: stderr only (line 47: `>&2`); no stdout of its own (though bash's own error banner, if $target is flag-shaped, leaks to stdout as a side effect — see main surface).
inventory.exit_code_notes: Always exit 0 after this message (line 48) — deliberate fail-open, explicitly justified in the header (design note (a), lines 19-22) as preventing a broken hook from bricking every Bash/Edit call.
error_samples: [hook-guard] WARN: PreToolUse hook /path/to/hook.sh failed to parse (bash -n) — failing OPEN (allowing tool); repair the hook, retrying will not help
evidence: .claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh:23-26 (comment, design note (b)) — explicitly scopes this to PARSE failures only ('bash -n'); a hook that parses cleanly but is semantically broken (missing sourced lib, etc.) is explicitly out of scope and gives NO warning at all — silently runs broken.
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: 'retrying will not help' is accurate for a genuine syntax error but is stated unconditionally even in the --help-probe case, where the real issue (wrong invocation, not a broken hook file) has nothing to do with 'repairing a hook'

inventory.name: $1 (target hook script path)
inventory.description: The sole positional argument: path to the wrapped safety-hook script. All remaining args ($2, $3, ...) are passed through untouched to the wrapped hook via `exec ... "$@"` after the shift.
inventory.help_probe: probe skipped: covered by the --help probe on the whole-script surface above; no separate flag-parsing logic exists to probe independently — the script has no flags, only this one positional arg.

inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a.
inventory.exit_code_notes: No validation that $1 is non-empty or an existing file before use (line 43-44: `target="$1"; shift`) — an empty/missing $1 flows straight into `bash -n ""`, which bash treats as a request to read a script from... an empty filename, producing a bash-internal error, caught by the same fail-open branch (exit 0) as any other parse failure. No dedicated 'missing required argument' message exists.
evidence: .claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh:46 — `bash -n "$target"` is the first point $target is actually used/validated, and only for parseability, never existence per se (a nonexistent path also fails `bash -n` cleanly into the same WARN+exit0 branch, so this particular gap has no distinct failure mode of its own — noted for completeness).

inventory.name: hook-guard.sh exit code contract
inventory.description: hook-guard.sh itself has exactly one direct exit path (0, on parse failure); every other outcome is determined entirely by whatever the wrapped hook (via `exec`) exits.
inventory.help_probe: probe skipped: derived from static reading, confirmed by both live probes above (both terminated 0).
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a (aggregate).
inventory.exit_code_notes: hook-guard.sh's own code contributes exactly ONE outcome (exit 0 on `bash -n` failure, line 48); the `exec` on line 51 REPLACES the process, so any other exit code an agent observes (e.g. 2 from block-destructive-bash.sh) is 100% attributable to the wrapped hook, never to hook-guard.sh itself — this invariant is stated in the header design notes but not observable from outside without reading source.
evidence: .claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh:46-51 — the only two outcomes: `exit 0` (line 48) or `exec ... "$@"` (line 51, which replaces the process image so this line's own 'return' never happens).
.claude/hooks/hook-guard.sh:9-16 (comment) — 'stdin/stdout/stderr/exit-code pass through byte-for-byte' claim for the successful-exec path, matching the code.

inventory.name: $BASH (interpreter selection)
inventory.description: Used twice (lines 46, 51) as `"${BASH:-bash}"` to select which bash binary both syntax-checks and then execs the wrapped hook — defaults to whatever `bash` resolves to on PATH if the $BASH env var (normally auto-set by bash itself to its own absolute path) is unset.
inventory.help_probe: probe skipped: env var, not independently invocable.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: n/a.
inventory.exit_code_notes: If $BASH is set to a broken/nonexistent path by the calling environment, both the `bash -n` check (line 46) and the `exec` (line 51) would fail — the `-n` failure funnels into the normal WARN+exit0 fail-open branch (line 47-48), so even a broken $BASH degrades safely for the CHECK; but note the `exec` on line 51 only runs after that check SUCCEEDED with the SAME `$BASH`, so this particular failure mode is self-consistent (same binary for both), not a split-brain risk.
worst_suspects: determinism_and_reproducibility: relies on the calling harness having set $BASH consistently with whatever `bash` resolves to on PATH; the two are not guaranteed identical in every environment (e.g. a PATH override that shadows `bash` differently from the process's own $BASH), a theoretical split not exercised by either live probe here

inventory.name: construct-resolve.sh
inventory.description: Entry point agents use to resolve a construct by slug/name/command, check write/read path composability between two constructs, list all constructs, or show a construct's aggregated capabilities. Sourced by construct-aware sections of CLAUDE.md/CLAUDE.loa.md when '.run/construct-index.yaml' exists.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage text (18 lines) to STDERR only — stdout was empty when captured separately (`2>/dev/null` produced zero bytes). Verified live: `bash .claude/scripts/construct-resolve.sh --help 2>/dev/null` -> empty stdout, exit 0; `1>/dev/null` -> full usage block, exit 0.
inventory.json_mode: partial: --json flag on resolve/compose/list/capabilities; the bare --help usage block itself is plain text only, no --json variant
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Usage/help text is written via `cat >&2` inside `_usage()` (construct-resolve.sh:113-130), so it lands on stderr even on the success path (exit 0). No tput/ANSI/color codes found anywhere in the file (grep for tput/\033/\e[ returned no real hits). Data-mode output (resolve/list/capabilities/compose results) correctly goes to stdout via jq/echo; only errors and usage go to stderr.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Documented dictionary in header comment (construct-resolve.sh:14-19) and repeated in usage text (construct-resolve.sh:127-129): 0=success, 1=no match/no overlap/not found, 2=collision (ambiguous match, first result still returned), 3=index missing/unreadable. Distinct codes are actually used, not a blanket exit 1 — verified by reading _resolve/_compose/_load_index return/exit statements.
error_samples: "ERROR: Unknown subcommand: $SUBCOMMAND" (construct-resolve.sh:96)
"ERROR: Construct index not found: $index_file" (construct-resolve.sh:142)
"ERROR: yq required to read YAML index" (construct-resolve.sh:151-152)
"ERROR: Index missing 'constructs' key" (construct-resolve.sh:163)
evidence: construct-resolve.sh:113-130 (_usage uses cat >&2, i.e. all usage text to stderr)
live probe: `bash construct-resolve.sh --help 2>/dev/null` empty stdout, exit 0; `1>/dev/null` shows usage, exit 0
worst_suspects: self_documentation: --help exits 0 (success) but the usage text it prints goes entirely to stderr, which is the convention for errors — an agent that greps stdout for 'Usage:' after a --help call to confirm the interface finds nothing and may misdiagnose the probe as having failed silently.

inventory.name: resolve <query>
inventory.description: Look up a construct by exact slug, case-insensitive name, or claimed command name across three tiers; the primary way an agent turns a user-typed construct reference into a canonical index entry.
inventory.help_probe: not independently probed (probing would call _load_index and require a real index file / touch state); usage for this subcommand is only surfaced via the top-level --help block (construct-resolve.sh:118), which the script itself prints — no per-subcommand `resolve --help`.
inventory.json_mode: yes: --json (construct-resolve.sh:225-229 for no-match case; _output_match at 233-246 for match case)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Match results and the JSON no-match envelope go to stdout via jq/echo (construct-resolve.sh:226,228,238-245). Ambiguity WARNINGs go to stderr (construct-resolve.sh:187,204,219) — correct stdout/stderr separation for this subcommand.
inventory.exit_code_notes: 0 = exactly one match at any tier (172-231), 2 = multiple matches at a tier (collision — first match still emitted), 1 = no match at any of the 3 tiers. Tier is reported in JSON (`tier`: slug/name/command) and in human text (`[matched by $tier]`, construct-resolve.sh:244).
error_samples: "WARNING: Multiple constructs with slug '$query'" (construct-resolve.sh:187)
"WARNING: Multiple constructs matching name '$query'" (construct-resolve.sh:204)
"WARNING: Multiple constructs claim command '$query'" (construct-resolve.sh:219)
evidence: construct-resolve.sh:64-71 (arg parsing: 'ERROR: resolve requires a <query> argument' at line 66, exit 1)
worst_suspects: intent_inference: on collision (exit 2) the script silently picks the FIRST jq-array match as the resolved entry with no documented ordering guarantee (index file order), so an agent retrying the same ambiguous query can get a different silently-chosen construct if the index is regenerated in a different order — non-deterministic resolution disguised as a definite answer.

inventory.name: compose <source> <target>
inventory.description: Checks whether two constructs can compose by testing prefix-overlap between source's writes/target's reads (and reverse); used before chaining constructs so an agent doesn't blindly assume write/read compatibility.
inventory.help_probe: not independently probed; documented only in top-level usage line 119 and header comment lines 9-10.
inventory.json_mode: yes: --json (construct-resolve.sh:305-322)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Composability result + overlap list go to stdout (construct-resolve.sh:305-325). Not-found errors for source/target go to stderr (261-274). A composition check is ALSO always appended to `.run/audit.jsonl` as a side effect (construct-resolve.sh:302, `_audit_log`) — an agent calling `compose` purely to introspect (read-only intent) still mutates state on disk; failures of that audit write are swallowed (`>> "$audit_file" 2>/dev/null || true`, line 465) so a broken audit path never surfaces to the caller.
inventory.exit_code_notes: 0 = overlap exists (composable), 1 = no overlap OR source/target not found (three different failure conditions share exit 1 — construct-resolve.sh:263,272,326). An agent cannot distinguish 'construct doesn't exist' from 'constructs exist but don't compose' by exit code alone; must parse stderr text.
error_samples: "ERROR: Source construct not found: $source_slug" (construct-resolve.sh:262)
"ERROR: Target construct not found: $target_slug" (construct-resolve.sh:272)
"No composition path: $source_slug -> $target_slug (no overlap)" (construct-resolve.sh:324, stdout not stderr)
evidence: construct-resolve.sh:72-80 (arg count check, 'ERROR: compose requires <source-slug> and <target-slug>' line 74, exit 1)
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: 'not found' (missing construct) and 'no overlap' (both constructs valid but incompatible) are two semantically distinct outcomes that collapse to the same exit code 1, forcing an agent to string-match stderr text to branch correctly instead of trusting the exit-code contract documented in the header.

inventory.name: --json
inventory.description: Switches every subcommand's stdout from human-readable text to a machine-parseable JSON envelope — the flag an agent should always pass when consuming output programmatically.
inventory.help_probe: documented in usage text only (construct-resolve.sh:124); no standalone probe applicable (it's a modifier, not runnable alone without a subcommand).
inventory.json_mode: yes: --json (this is the toggle for it)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: When set, all four subcommands emit a distinct JSON shape (resolved/construct/tier for resolve; composable/overlapping_paths for compose; array of slugs for list; slug/capabilities for capabilities) — shapes are NOT unified under one schema, so an agent parsing generically must branch on subcommand.
inventory.exit_code_notes: --json does not change exit codes, only output formatting (verified by reading all _output_match/_compose/_list/_capabilities branches — same return/exit values on both paths).
error_samples: JSON no-match envelope: `{"resolved": false, "query": $q, "error": "no match"}` (construct-resolve.sh:226)
worst_suspects: composability: each subcommand's --json schema is bespoke (different top-level keys, no shared envelope like {ok,data,error}), so a generic 'always parse with --json' wrapper an agent writes once still needs per-subcommand key knowledge — undermines the promise that --json alone is enough to script against this tool uniformly.

inventory.name: --index PATH
inventory.description: Overrides the construct index file location for a single invocation, taking priority over both the CONSTRUCT_INDEX_PATH env var and the default '.run/construct-index.yaml' path.
inventory.help_probe: documented in usage text (construct-resolve.sh:125); not independently probed since it requires a valid index argument.
inventory.json_mode: no (flag itself carries no format; combine with --json)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A — flag value only affects which file _load_index reads (construct-resolve.sh:139).
inventory.exit_code_notes: If PATH doesn't exist, downstream _load_index exits 3 with 'ERROR: Construct index not found: $index_file' (construct-resolve.sh:142-144).
error_samples: "ERROR: Unknown flag: $1" (construct-resolve.sh:108) — thrown if --index is passed without a following value only in the sense that `$2` would be consumed unchecked; a missing value after `--index` at end-of-args triggers bash unbound-ish empty assignment (INDEX_PATH="") rather than an explicit error, since `set -u` still lets `${2:-}`-less `$2` produce an empty string here (construct-resolve.sh:106 does `INDEX_PATH="$2"; shift 2` with no existence check on $2)
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery: `--index` with no following value silently shifts past the end of args instead of erroring immediately at parse time, deferring the failure to the unrelated-looking 'Construct index not found' message downstream, which doesn't mention the flag was the cause.

inventory.name: CONSTRUCT_INDEX_PATH
inventory.description: Session-wide override for the index file location, used when an agent wants every construct-resolve.sh call in a session to target a non-default index without repeating --index on each call.
inventory.help_probe: documented in header comment only (construct-resolve.sh:21); not shown in the --help usage block itself (usage text omits env vars entirely — asymmetry between header docstring and runtime usage output).
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A (env var, not output).
inventory.exit_code_notes: Same downstream exit 3 path as --index if the resolved file is missing (construct-resolve.sh:139-144).
error_samples: "ERROR: Construct index not found: $index_file" (construct-resolve.sh:142)
worst_suspects: self_documentation: the runtime `--help`/usage output (construct-resolve.sh:113-130) never mentions CONSTRUCT_INDEX_PATH or PROJECT_ROOT at all — an agent that only reads the live --help text (rather than the source header) has no way to discover these env vars exist.

inventory.description: Signals the index file itself is missing, unreadable, unparseable, or malformed (no 'constructs' key) — distinguishes infra/setup failures from ordinary 'not found' results (exit 1), letting an agent decide whether to bootstrap the index vs. try a different query.
inventory.help_probe: documented in header (construct-resolve.sh:18) and usage text (construct-resolve.sh:129).
inventory.json_mode: no (index-load errors are always plain stderr text, even when --json was requested — --json only takes effect after _load_index succeeds, since main() calls _load_index before dispatching to subcommands, construct-resolve.sh:472-481)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: All 4 distinct exit-3 error paths write to stderr only (construct-resolve.sh:142,151,155,163); nothing goes to stdout on this path even when --json was passed, so a --json-consuming agent gets an empty/absent JSON body on this failure class and must fall back to stderr text.
inventory.exit_code_notes: One code, four distinct causes: file missing (142), yq not installed (151), yq parse failure (155), missing 'constructs' key post-parse (163). All indistinguishable by exit code alone.
error_samples: "ERROR: Construct index not found: $index_file" (construct-resolve.sh:142)
"ERROR: yq required to read YAML index" (construct-resolve.sh:151)
"ERROR: Failed to parse index: $index_file" (construct-resolve.sh:156)
"ERROR: Index missing 'constructs' key" (construct-resolve.sh:163)
worst_suspects: output_parseability: even with --json requested, every exit-3 failure mode is plain unstructured stderr text rather than a JSON error envelope — an agent that only parses stdout-as-JSON on every call gets nothing to parse on this whole failure class and must maintain a separate stderr-text-matching path just for index-load errors.

inventory.name: ERROR: Unknown subcommand
inventory.description: Fires when an agent guesses a subcommand name that doesn't exist — including the natural guess 'help' (as opposed to '-h'/'--help'), which is the exact case this audit was asked to probe.
inventory.help_probe: confirmed live: `bash construct-resolve.sh help` -> stderr: "ERROR: Unknown subcommand: help" followed by the full usage block (also stderr), exit 1. Contrast with `--help`/`-h` which exit 0 and print ONLY the usage block (no error line).
inventory.json_mode: no — this error path never checks JSON_OUTPUT because it fires inside _parse_args before JSON_OUTPUT could even be considered relevant (construct-resolve.sh:95-99)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Both the error line and the subsequent usage dump go to stderr (construct-resolve.sh:96-97, _usage uses cat>&2); stdout is empty on this path — verified live.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Exit 1 — same code as 'no match' from resolve/compose, so exit code alone can't tell an agent 'you mistyped the subcommand' apart from 'the subcommand ran fine but found nothing'.
error_samples: "ERROR: Unknown subcommand: $SUBCOMMAND" (construct-resolve.sh:96)
evidence: live probe: `bash construct-resolve.sh help` -> "ERROR: Unknown subcommand: help" + usage, exit 1 (stdout empty, confirmed via 2>/dev/null vs 1>/dev/null split)
worst_suspects: agent_intuitiveness: 'help' is the single most natural word an agent or human would try after '-h'/'--help' both work, yet it is the one spelling that produces an ERROR line instead of being treated as a help request — a foreseeable trap given the other two conventional spellings are supported.

inventory.name: list
inventory.description: Enumerates all known construct slugs from the index — cheapest, side-effect-free discovery call an agent makes to see what's installed before resolve/compose/capabilities.
inventory.help_probe: not independently probed (requires a valid index file to be meaningful and non-trivial to run safely under the read-only Bash allowlist); documented at construct-resolve.sh:120.
inventory.json_mode: yes: --json emits a JSON array of slugs vs. newline-separated plain slugs otherwise (construct-resolve.sh:407-411)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: All output to stdout via jq/echo (construct-resolve.sh:408,410); no stderr output on the success path; no failure path exists for `list` itself beyond the shared _load_index exit-3 errors.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Always returns 0 (construct-resolve.sh:412) — even an empty index (0 constructs) still exits 0 with an empty list/array, giving no signal that the index might be degenerate.
error_samples: N/A — list has no subcommand-specific error path; only the shared _load_index errors (see exit_code::3-index-missing) can fire before _list runs
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: an empty index (constructs: [] or a corrupted-but-parseable file) and a healthy index with real entries are both exit 0 with no warning distinguishing 'this worked and found nothing' from 'this worked and found things' — an agent must count array length itself to notice zero results.

inventory.name: capabilities <slug>
inventory.description: Shows a specific construct's aggregated_capabilities map — used by an agent deciding whether a construct can perform a given action before invoking it.
inventory.help_probe: not independently probed; documented at construct-resolve.sh:121.
inventory.json_mode: yes: --json (construct-resolve.sh:434-436)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Result to stdout (construct-resolve.sh:435-439); not-found error to stderr (construct-resolve.sh:427).
inventory.exit_code_notes: 0 on found (construct-resolve.sh:441), 1 on construct not found (construct-resolve.sh:428) — but ALSO silently returns 0 with an empty `{}` capabilities map if the slug exists but has no `aggregated_capabilities` key at all (construct-resolve.sh:432, `// {}` default) — 'construct has zero capabilities' and 'construct exists but capability data was never populated' are indistinguishable.
error_samples: "ERROR: Construct not found: $slug" (construct-resolve.sh:427)
evidence: construct-resolve.sh:83-90 (arg check, 'ERROR: capabilities requires a <slug> argument' line 85, exit 1)
worst_suspects: intent_inference: the `// {}` jq default (construct-resolve.sh:432) silently masks missing capability data behind the same empty-map shape as 'this construct genuinely has no capabilities', so an agent can't tell whether to trust the negative result or suspect an index-population bug.

inventory.name: session-limit-capture.sh
inventory.description: Called (per CLAUDE.loa.md) whenever an agent observes a Claude session/usage-cap error in a tool or subagent result; parses the reset time out of the raw error text and snapshots live run/bridge/simstim state into .run/session-limit-state.json so a later hook can inject a one-shot resume reminder.
inventory.help_probe: no --help handling at all. Live probes: `--help`, `-h`, and literal `help` all fall through the arg-parsing loop's catch-all `*) shift ;;` (session-limit-capture.sh:34) with zero acknowledgment of the flag, leaving RAW empty; the script then hits the same generic empty-RAW check and prints "session-limit-capture: --raw '<error text>' is required" to stderr, exit 1 — identical output to running with literally no args at all. Confirmed live for --help/-h/help/no-args, all four: same stderr line, exit 1, empty stdout.
inventory.json_mode: no — output is a hand-built jq object written to a FILE (.run/session-limit-state.json), never printed to stdout; there is no --json flag and no stdout data mode at all
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Every message this script ever emits — success confirmation included — goes to stderr, never stdout: the success line "session-limit-capture: wrote $STATE_FILE (reset_at=$RESET_ISO)" is itself `>&2` (session-limit-capture.sh:130). Stdout is unconditionally empty on every path (success or failure), verified live for the --help/no-args probes (`2>/dev/null` produced zero bytes, exit still reported by the outer echo). No tput/ANSI usage found.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Blanket exit 1 for every failure class (missing --raw, unrecognized cap string, unparseable reset time, unparseable epoch, non-numeric epoch, snapshot-JSON build failure, atomic-write failure) — 7 distinct stderr messages all collapse to the same exit code 1; only exit 0 (successful capture) is distinguishable, and even that isn't visible on stdout, only inferable from the process exit status plus a stderr string match.
error_samples: "session-limit-capture: --raw '<error text>' is required" (session-limit-capture.sh:39) — verified live for --help/-h/help/no-args
"session-limit-capture: input is not a recognized session-limit string; nothing captured" (session-limit-capture.sh:44)
"session-limit-capture: could not parse reset time (GNU-date-only; see session-limit-lib.sh)" (session-limit-capture.sh:49)
"session-limit-capture: failed to write $STATE_FILE" (session-limit-capture.sh:134)
evidence: session-limit-capture.sh:19-20 (header: documents exit 1 conditions but never mentions --help)
session-limit-capture.sh:30-36 (arg loop; --help/-h/help all hit the catch-all `*) shift` with no error or usage)
session-limit-capture.sh:38-41 (empty-RAW check, the message every unrecognized-flag probe converges on)
live probe: `bash session-limit-capture.sh --help/-h/help` and no-args all four produced identical stderr text and exit 1, stdout empty in every case
worst_suspects: self_documentation: there is no help surface whatsoever — --help, -h, and help are all silently swallowed by the generic flag loop and produce a generic 'missing --raw' message with zero indication of what a valid raw string looks like, what session_limit_matches() actually recognizes, or that a resume-reminder hook is the consumer of this state file; an agent's only path to understanding the contract is reading the script source or its sibling lib file.

inventory.name: --raw '<error text>'
inventory.description: The only real input to this script — the full verbatim session-limit/usage-cap error text an agent observed, which the script parses for a reset time via session_limit_matches/session_limit_parse_reset (from lib/session-limit-lib.sh).
inventory.help_probe: no dedicated help; --raw is documented only in the header comment (session-limit-capture.sh:7) and inferred from the 'is required' error message at runtime. Both `--raw VALUE` (two-arg) and `--raw=VALUE` (single-token) forms are accepted (session-limit-capture.sh:32-33); an agent guessing either spelling gets consistent behavior.
inventory.json_mode: no (plain string argument, not JSON)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A — input flag, not output. Downstream validation errors for a badly-formed --raw value are all stderr-only (see error_samples).
inventory.exit_code_notes: Exit 1 whenever --raw is absent (39-40), unrecognized as a cap string (43-45), or has an unparseable reset time/epoch (48-59) — four distinct validation failures on this one flag, all exit 1, no differentiation.
error_samples: "session-limit-capture: input is not a recognized session-limit string; nothing captured" (session-limit-capture.sh:44)
"session-limit-capture: could not parse reset epoch" (session-limit-capture.sh:53)
"session-limit-capture: parsed reset epoch is not numeric" (session-limit-capture.sh:57)
worst_suspects: error_pedagogy: 'input is not a recognized session-limit string' (line 44) never shows the agent what pattern WAS expected nor a pointer to session_limit_matches()'s actual regex — an agent whose captured error text is 99% right (e.g. slightly different phrasing than what Claude Code emits) gets no diff/near-miss feedback to self-correct with, only a flat rejection.

inventory.name: PROJECT_ROOT
inventory.description: Overrides where .run/session-limit-state.json is written/read relative to; defaults to $PWD, so an agent invoking this script from a different cwd than the repo root must set it explicitly or the state file lands in the wrong place.
inventory.help_probe: not documented anywhere in --help/usage (there is none) or in the header comment; only discoverable by reading source line 63.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A (env var).
inventory.exit_code_notes: No dedicated validation of PROJECT_ROOT — if it points to a non-writable or nonexistent path, the failure surfaces later and generically as "session-limit-capture: failed to write $STATE_FILE" (line 134), not as a PROJECT_ROOT-specific complaint.
error_samples: "session-limit-capture: failed to write $STATE_FILE" (session-limit-capture.sh:134)
worst_suspects: self_documentation: PROJECT_ROOT is a silent default-to-$PWD fallback with zero mention in any user-facing text (no --help exists to mention it in, and the header docstring omits it) — an agent running this script from a subdirectory or a worktree has no signal it should set PROJECT_ROOT until state ends up in the wrong .run/ directory.

inventory.name: 1 (undifferentiated)
inventory.description: The single failure exit code this script ever returns, covering seven semantically distinct failure causes an agent must otherwise distinguish by string-matching stderr.
inventory.help_probe: probe skipped for triggering the write-failure branch (would require manipulating .run/ permissions, outside the read-only/help-only allowlist); the other 6 causes (missing --raw / bad cap-string / unparseable reset / unparseable epoch / non-numeric epoch / jq build failure) were reachable via the permitted --help/-h/help/no-args probes, all converging on the same 'missing --raw' branch and confirming exit 1.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: All 7 failure messages are stderr-only text (session-limit-capture.sh:39,44,49,53,57,121,134); stdout is empty on every failure path, matching the live probe results.
inventory.exit_code_notes: No documented dictionary of distinct codes (contrast with construct-resolve.sh's 0/1/2/3 table) — the header docstring only says 'Exit 1 ... when --raw is not a recognized cap string or its reset time cannot be parsed' (session-limit-capture.sh:19-20), which itself undercounts the actual number of exit-1 branches in the code (7, not 2).
error_samples: "session-limit-capture: --raw '<error text>' is required" (session-limit-capture.sh:39)
"session-limit-capture: failed to build snapshot JSON (jq unavailable?)" (session-limit-capture.sh:121)
"session-limit-capture: failed to write $STATE_FILE" (session-limit-capture.sh:134)
evidence: session-limit-capture.sh:19-20 (header only documents 2 of the 7 causes)
worst_suspects: determinism_and_reproducibility: an agent scripting a retry-vs-give-up decision around this call has no exit-code signal to distinguish a transient/environmental failure (jq missing, disk write failure) from a permanent one (malformed input text) — both are exit 1, so a blind retry-on-failure loop would futilely re-run against unparseable input forever, while a give-up-on-failure loop would never retry a transient disk hiccup.

inventory.name: mount-loa.sh
inventory.description: Top-level installer entrypoint. Default (no flags) performs a full submodule-mode mount: acquires a PID lock, clones/updates the .loa submodule, syncs zones, writes config/manifest/checksums, and creates a git commit. Also curl|bash bootstraps itself (re-execs after downloading sibling scripts to a temp dir) when BASH_SOURCE[0] is unset.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage to stdout, 34 lines. Ran `timeout 10 bash .claude/scripts/mount-loa.sh --help` after static-proving compat-lib.sh (unconditionally sourced at mount-loa.sh:99 before arg parsing) has zero mutating top-level side effects (uname/sort/sed/readlink/find/stat probes only, all read-only) and the -h|--help case (mount-loa.sh:374-416) is hit and `exit 0`s inside the arg-parsing while loop, strictly before preflight()/main() ever run.
inventory.json_mode: partial: only verify_mount() and post_mount_constructs_install() support --json/structured output; the top-level mount flow is text-only with an embedded JSON error envelope on stderr via mount_error()
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Data (usage text, log()/step()/info()) goes to stdout; err()/mount_error()/warn() go to stderr. Heavy ANSI color usage (RED/GREEN/YELLOW/CYAN/BLUE/NC at mount-loa.sh:102-107) via printf '%b' with NO tty check (`[[ -t 1 ]]` or `tput` gate) anywhere in the file — colors are emitted unconditionally into any pipe/log capture, requiring agents to strip ANSI or set NO_COLOR themselves.
inventory.exit_code_notes: No single documented exit-code dictionary for the whole script; only the E0xx mount_error() codes (E010-E016) are enumerated (mount-loa.sh:147-156), each of which calls exit 1 uniformly — so all structured errors collapse to process exit 1, distinguishable only by parsing the JSON `code` field on stderr. Non-structured failures (e.g. `set -e` on an unexpected command) fall through the EXIT trap (mount-loa.sh:253-271) which also exits with whatever the underlying command's code was.
error_samples: 'ERROR: invalid ref '\''%s'\'' (must match [A-Za-z0-9._/-]+)' (mount-loa.sh:44, pipe-bootstrap ref validation)
'Loa already installed. Use --force flag to remount: curl ... | bash -s -- --force' (mount-loa.sh:479, non-interactive stdin + existing mount)
'Option $1 requires a value (got '\''${2:-}'\'')' (mount-loa.sh:298, require_operand guard for --branch/--tag/--ref/--constructs-pack)
'Submodule script not found at: $submodule_script\nPlease ensure Loa framework is complete or download mount-submodule.sh manually.' (mount-loa.sh:1656-1657)
worst_suspects: output_parseability: ANSI escapes unconditionally interleaved with data on stdout (no tty check), forcing every agent consumer to strip color codes before parsing log lines

inventory.name: help (bare word, no dash)
inventory.description: An agent naturally trying `bash mount-loa.sh help` (mirroring `git help`, `br help`, etc.) does NOT hit any help branch.
inventory.help_probe: probe skipped: source reading proves this is UNSAFE to execute. The arg-parsing case statement (mount-loa.sh:303-421) matches only `-h|--help` for help output; a bare `help` token falls to the wildcard `*) warn "Unknown option: $1"; shift ;;` (mount-loa.sh:417-420), is silently discarded, the loop then exits ($#==0), and execution falls through to `main "$@"` at mount-loa.sh:2301 — which performs a REAL mount (acquire_mount_lock, preflight, git remote add/fetch, git checkout from upstream, file writes, git commit, git tag). Running this probe would mutate the working repo, violating the hard safety rule.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A (not executed)
inventory.exit_code_notes: Would exit via main()'s normal path (0 on success) rather than the help path — no error surfaced to the agent about the typo at all.
error_samples: 'Unknown option: help' (mount-loa.sh:418) — printed as a WARNING, not an error; script proceeds anyway
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery: the single most natural agent guess for getting help (`<script> help`) silently degrades into full destructive execution instead of erroring or showing usage
intent_inference: an unrecognized token is treated as a no-op warning rather than inferred as a help request or rejected as invalid input

inventory.name: --force / -f
inventory.description: Forces remount without the interactive y/N prompt; required for any non-interactive (agent) invocation once Loa is already mounted, since the alternative path is a `read -p` that blocks forever with no stdin.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, documented in usage stdout (line 'Force remount without prompting')
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A — flag only gates control flow
inventory.exit_code_notes: Without --force AND with `[[ -t 0 ]]` false (agent/non-tty context), preflight() calls `err "Loa already installed. Use --force flag..."` → exit 1 (mount-loa.sh:479). With a tty and no --force, `read -p` blocks awaiting y/N — a hazard for any agent harness that doesn't detect this and pass --force.
error_samples: 'Loa already installed. Use --force flag to remount: curl ... | bash -s -- --force' (mount-loa.sh:479)
worst_suspects: agent_ergonomics: interactive-tty branch uses a blocking `read -p` with no timeout; an agent running under a pty without recognizing this pattern hangs indefinitely rather than failing fast

inventory.name: --migrate-to-submodule [--apply]
inventory.description: Dry-run (default) or --apply (executes) migration of a vendored install to submodule mode: classifies .claude/ files as framework/user-modified/user-owned via checksums.json, backs up, git rm --cached, submodule add, recreate symlinks, commit.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, documented in usage stdout ('Migration:' section)
inventory.json_mode: no — human-readable classification report only (info/warn lines), no --json flag for this subcommand despite its report-like structure
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: All classification report lines go through info()/warn() (stdout/stderr per the log helper conventions) but the report itself (framework/user_modified/user_owned file lists) has no machine-parseable delimiter — an agent must regex the human-readable headers to extract file lists.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Dry run always exits 0 (mount-loa.sh:1980) whether or not files are classified as risky; --apply exits 0 on success, or via err() (exit 1) on dirty tree / no .loa-version.json / feasibility failure. No distinct exit code differentiates 'dry-run complete' from 'nothing to migrate' (both exit 0).
error_samples: 'Working tree is dirty. Please commit or stash changes first:\n  git stash\nThen re-run: mount-loa.sh --migrate-to-submodule --apply' (mount-loa.sh:1828-1831)
'No .loa-version.json found. Is Loa installed?' (mount-loa.sh:1814)
'Migration cannot proceed. Fix the above issues and retry.' (mount-loa.sh:1895, after disk-space/permissions/network feasibility failures)
worst_suspects: output_parseability: the classification report (framework/user_modified/user_owned lists) is prose-formatted with no JSON/delimited mode, unlike verify_mount()'s --json support elsewhere in the same file

inventory.name: --vendored
inventory.description: Switches from the default submodule-mode install to legacy vendored mode (copies framework files directly into .claude/ instead of symlinking to a .loa/ submodule).
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, documented in usage stdout ('Installation Modes:' section)
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A
inventory.exit_code_notes: check_mode_conflicts() (mount-loa.sh:1519-1543) hard-errors (exit 1 via err()) if the repo is already mounted in the OTHER mode — the error message embeds a 3-4 step manual recovery procedure rather than an automatable flag.
error_samples:
'Loa is installed in submodule mode. Cannot switch to vendored mode.
To switch manually:
  1. Remove the submodule: git submodule deinit -f .loa && git rm -f .loa
  2. Remove symlinks: rm -rf .claude
  3. Remove .loa-version.json and .loa.config.yaml
  4. Run mount-loa.sh --vendored' (mount-loa.sh:1535-1540)
worst_suspects: agent_ergonomics: mode-switch recovery is a manual multi-step shell procedure printed as prose, not an automatable flag/subcommand an agent could invoke directly

inventory.name: LOA_UPSTREAM / LOA_BRANCH
inventory.description: Override the upstream git remote URL and branch used for both the vendored sync (git fetch/checkout) and the submodule add. Primary lever for pointing an agent's mount at a fork or pinned branch without CLI flags.
inventory.help_probe: no --help handling for env vars: usage text never documents LOA_UPSTREAM/LOA_BRANCH at all (only --branch/--tag/--ref flags are documented in usage output) — an agent must read source to discover these override points.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A
inventory.exit_code_notes: Invalid/unreachable LOA_UPSTREAM surfaces indirectly through `git fetch "$LOA_REMOTE_NAME" "$LOA_BRANCH" --quiet` failing under `set -e`, which is caught only by the generic EXIT trap (E013 unexpected-failure JSON), not a dedicated error code — the underlying git stderr is not classified or surfaced distinctly from any other unexpected failure.
error_samples: 'ERROR (E013): Unexpected failure (exit code 1)' (mount-loa.sh:265, generic catch-all for e.g. an unreachable LOA_UPSTREAM)
evidence: .claude/scripts/mount-loa.sh:374-416 (help text omits these env vars entirely)
worst_suspects: self_documentation: two of the most consequential override points (which upstream repo/branch gets fetched and merged into the working tree) are entirely absent from --help output

inventory.name: mount_error() E010-E016 structured codes
inventory.description: The only place mount-loa.sh gives a stable, documented error taxonomy: E010 (no git), E011 (empty-repo commit failed), E012 (git user not configured), E013 (generic commit failure / unexpected), E014 (staging failed), E015 (bare repo), E016 (commit-policy detected, non-fatal warning variant).
inventory.help_probe: no --help handling: this dictionary is not surfaced via any --help/--list-errors flag; it exists only as inline `case` logic (mount-loa.sh:147-156) discoverable solely by reading source.
inventory.json_mode: yes: --json — each mount_error() call emits a `{"code":...,"name":...,"message":...,"fix":...}` (or with `"details"`) JSON object to stderr in addition to the colored human text (mount-loa.sh:172-177)
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Both the human-readable and the JSON envelope go to stderr (mount-loa.sh:158-177) — none of it is on stdout, so an agent piping stdout only will miss error detail entirely and see just the exit code.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Every code in the dictionary maps to exit 1 uniformly (mount_error always `exit 1` at line 180) — E010 through E016 are NOT distinguishable via $? alone; an agent must parse the stderr JSON's `code` field to know which of the 7 failure classes occurred. E016 alone is non-fatal (mount_warn_policy returns 0) despite sharing the same code namespace.
error_samples: '{"code":"E012","name":"mount_git_user_not_configured","message":"git user.name or user.email not set","fix":"Run: git config user.name ..."}' (mount-loa.sh:172-176)
'{"code":"E015","name":"mount_bare_repo","message":"Repository is bare (no working tree)",...}' (mount-loa.sh:153)
worst_suspects: determinism_and_reproducibility: 7 distinct documented failure classes all collapse to the same process exit code (1); the differentiator (JSON `code` field) lives only on stderr, not reflected in $?

inventory.name: mount-submodule.sh

Adds .loa/ as a git submodule, symlinks .claude/* into it from a shared manifest, updates .gitignore, writes config/manifest, and commits.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, usage to stdout, 15 lines. Only prior side effect is `umask 077` (line 28, process-local, non-mutating).
inventory.json_mode: no — entirely human-readable log()/step()/warn() output; no --json flag exists anywhere in this script (contrast with mount-loa.sh's verify_mount --json)

inventory.help_probe: exit 0, documented in usage stdout ('Check symlink health (no changes)')
inventory.json_mode: no — warn()/log() prose lines only (DANGLING/MISSING/COPY-STALE/etc. as free-text prefixes), no --json companion despite being the one surface that's naturally a status-check API
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Health-check findings (DANGLING/MISSING/DEGRADED/COPY-STALE/COPY-DRIFT/MARKER-STALE) all go through warn() → stderr; the final 'All symlinks healthy.'/'Symlink issues detected...' summary via log()/warn() also stderr-or-stdout depending on outcome — an agent capturing only stdout for a health check would miss most of the actual diagnostic content.
inventory.exit_code_notes: `exit $result` where result is 0 (healthy) or 1 (any dangling/missing symlink OR any copy-set issue) — binary only; cannot distinguish 'symlinks fine but copy-set drifted' from 'symlinks badly broken' via exit code alone (both are 1). Version-marker staleness is deliberately WARN-only and never flips the exit code (mount-submodule.sh:1139-1142).
error_samples: '  DANGLING: $link_path' (mount-submodule.sh:1012)
'  COPY-DRIFT: $dest content differs from $target — run --reconcile' (mount-submodule.sh:928)
'  MARKER-STALE: .loa-version.json framework_version '$marker_version' != submodule pin '$actual_version' — run update-loa.sh' (mount-submodule.sh:1115)
worst_suspects: output_parseability: the one clearly status/health-check-shaped surface in either script has no --json mode, forcing regex parsing of DANGLING/MISSING/COPY-* prefixed warn() lines

inventory.name: --reconcile
inventory.description: Mutating fix-up companion to --check-symlinks: recreates dangling/missing symlinks from the manifest and refreshes the #842 copy-set (hooks/, settings.json) in place.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, documented in usage stdout ('Check and fix symlink health')
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: Same warn()/log() mixed stdout/stderr pattern as --check-symlinks, plus FIXED/RESTORED/CREATED/Refreshed-copy confirmation lines.
inventory.exit_code_notes: `exit $reconcile_rc` — 1 if verify_and_reconcile_symlinks OR refresh_copy_set report any residual issue after attempting fixes (e.g. a target genuinely missing so the symlink can't be recreated), 0 otherwise. Partial-success (some fixed, some not) is only revealed by comparing 'fixed' vs 'dangling+stale' counts in the printed summary — not encoded distinctly in the exit code beyond binary success/failure.
error_samples: 'Reconcile partial failure: ${fixed} fixed but ${dangling} dangling + ${stale} missing remained' (mount-submodule.sh:1083)
'  COPY-SET: refusing non-.claude destination '$dest'' (mount-submodule.sh:899, deletion-surface guard)
worst_suspects: composability: no dry-run preview of exactly what --reconcile will change before it mutates — an agent must run --check-symlinks first and manually correlate its prose output with what --reconcile will then do

inventory.name: --force / -f
inventory.description: Bypasses the y/N remount confirmation AND, in add_submodule(), triggers destructive removal of an existing submodule (`git submodule deinit -f`, `git rm -f`, `rm -rf .git/modules/.loa`, `rm -rf .loa`) before re-adding it fresh.
inventory.help_probe: exit 0, documented in usage stdout ('Force remount without prompting')
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A
inventory.exit_code_notes: Without --force when a submodule already exists: `err "Submodule already exists. Use --force to remount."` → exit 1 (line 356). With --force, the prior submodule content is unconditionally destroyed with no confirmation and no dry-run — the flag conflates 'skip confirmation prompt' with 'destroy and recreate existing submodule data', a meaningfully different blast radius than mount-loa.sh's --force (which only skips a confirmation, never deletes).
error_samples: 'Submodule already exists. Use --force to remount.' (mount-submodule.sh:356)
worst_suspects: safety_with_recovery: --force silently destroys the existing .loa/ submodule's git history/working tree (deinit + rm -rf .git/modules) with zero preview, in a script whose --help text describes it only as 'Force remount without prompting'

inventory.name: --source-only
inventory.description: Undocumented (absent from --help output) escape hatch that lets another script `source` mount-submodule.sh purely to reuse its functions (get_effective_ref, validate_symlink_target, refresh_copy_set, etc.) without running main()'s mount sequence.
inventory.help_probe: no --help handling: this flag is entirely absent from the printed usage text (lines 109-127) despite being a real, load-bearing option (grep confirms no other reference to '--source-only' in usage strings) — only discoverable by reading the arg-parsing case statement.
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: N/A — causes main() to `return 0` immediately (mount-submodule.sh:1154-1156), suppressing all script output.
inventory.exit_code_notes: Always 0 when reached (a bare `return 0` from main()), regardless of what happens afterward in the sourcing script.
evidence: .claude/scripts/mount-submodule.sh:103-107 (flag def, comment: 'Allow sourcing this script for its functions without running main')
worst_suspects: self_documentation: a flag whose entire purpose is enabling safe reuse-by-sourcing is invisible in --help, so an agent must read source to discover the one supported way to import this script's functions without triggering a mount

inventory.name: Security: Symlink target escapes repository bounds
inventory.description: MED-004 hardening in validate_symlink_target(): refuses to create any symlink whose resolved target falls outside the repo root, preventing a crafted/relative target from linking .claude/ content outside the project tree.
inventory.help_probe: no --help handling: this is a runtime safety check, not a flag; undiscoverable except via source or by triggering it
inventory.json_mode: no
inventory.stdout_stderr_notes: err() → stderr, exit 1 (three lines: message, resolved target, repo root) — good multi-line diagnostic depth relative to the rest of the script's mostly single-line errors.
inventory.exit_code_notes: exit 1 via err(), same undifferentiated code as every other failure in this script.
error_samples: 'Security: Symlink target escapes repository bounds: $target' / '  Target resolves to: $resolved_target' / '  Repository root: $repo_root' (mount-submodule.sh:448-450)
worst_suspects: regression_resistance: the safety property (no symlink escaping repo bounds) is enforced only by string-prefix comparison against a realpath-resolved repo_root inside a single function called from every symlink creation site — a future call site that skips safe_symlink() (calls `ln -sf` directly) silently bypasses the guard with no lint/test enforcing that invariant

corpus.why_plausible: Script has no --help/-h handling at all (only --json/--verbose/--quick/--repair/--dry-run/--force); an agent naturally tries --help first to discover usage, but it falls into the catch-all 'Unknown option' branch and exits 1.
Transposed-letter typo of --json, a common fat-finger; hits 'Unknown option: --jsno' and exits 1.
Agent guesses a generic --format json flag (a common CLI convention) but this script only supports the bare --json flag; unrecognized, exits 1.
Agent mistakenly treats 'health' as a subcommand-style argument (echoing the script's own name); hits the catch-all unknown-option branch and exits 1.
Plausible guess at a shorthand for --from-tag; not defined, falls into the default case 'ERROR: Unknown argument' and exits 2.
Bare 'help' (no dashes), by analogy with subcommand-style CLIs; only --help/-h are parsed, so this hits the default unknown-argument case and exits 2.
Agent drops the 'from-' prefix by analogy to a simpler flag name; only --from-changelog is recognized, so this is unknown and exits 2.
Since the script's output is already JSON, an agent may add --json thinking it forces that format (a common convention elsewhere in the repo); not a recognized flag here, exits 2.
Typo of --json; the option loop's '-*)' catch-all reports 'Unknown option: --jsno' and exits 1.
Agent forgets the required <id> positional after --full; obs_id resolves empty and show_full's own check reports 'Error: Observation ID required' and exits 1.
Single-dash long-form confusion; '-help' matches the generic '-*)' catch-all rather than the '-h|--help' case, producing 'Unknown option: -help' and exiting 1.
Plausible nonexistent flag borrowed from sibling scripts (e.g. beads-health.sh has --verbose); memory-query.sh has no such flag and exits 1 via the catch-all.
Both --file and --gate are required; a bare invocation (expecting sane defaults like other read-only checkers) trips 'Error: --file and --gate are required' and exits 2.
Agent supplies only --file, forgetting the also-required --gate flag; hits the same required-args check and exits 2.
Typo'd gate value ('audti' instead of 'audit') passes the presence check but fails the enum check ('--gate must be review or audit') and exits 2.
Typo'd --file as --fiel; falls into the default 'Unknown option' case and exits 2 before the required-args check is even reached.
A subcommand is required ($# -lt 1 check); a bare invocation exits 1 via _usage.
